The annual carrying cost is 20% of the unit cost, and the unit cost is ₹50/kg.** **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Determine the EOQ, the optimal number of orders, and the minimum total variable inventory cost. **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** If the lead time is 12 days and the company maintains a safety stock of 400 kg, compute the reorder point and total cost including safety stock holding cost. --- #### Solution to Question B: **Given Data:** - $D = 24{,}000$ kg/year - $S = ₹600$ per order - $P = ₹50$ per kg - Carrying cost rate = **20%** of unit price **Step 1: Calculate Holding Cost (H)** $$H = 0.20 \times 50 = ₹10 \text{ per kg/year}$$ --- **Part (a) Solution:** **EOQ:** $$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 24{,}000 \times 600}{10}} = \sqrt{\frac{28{,}800{,}000}{10}} = \sqrt{2{,}880{,}000}$$ $$Q^* = 1697.06 \text{ kg} \approx 1697 \text{ kg}$$ **Optimal Number of Orders:** $$N = \frac{24{,}000}{1697.06} = 14.14 \approx 14 \text{ orders/year}$$ **Minimum Total Variable Cost:** $$TVC_{min} = \sqrt{2 \times 24{,}000 \times 600 \times 10}$$ $$TVC_{min} = \sqrt{288{,}000{,}000} = ₹16{,}970.56$$ --- **Part (b) Solution:** **Step 1: Average Daily Demand (d)** $$d = \frac{D}{365} = \frac{24{,}000}{365} = 65.75 \text{ kg/day}$$ **Step 2: Reorder Point (R)** $$R = (d \times L) + \text{Safety Stock}$$ $$R = (65.75 \times 12) + 400$$ $$R = 789.04 + 400 = 1189.04 \approx 1189 \text{ kg}$$ **Step 3: Holding Cost of Safety Stock** $$C_{SS} = \text{Safety Stock} \times H = 400 \times 10 = ₹4{,}000 \text{ per year}$$ **Step 4: New Total Cost (with Safety Stock)** The EOQ itself **does not change** with safety stock — only the reorder point and total carrying cost do. New Total Variable Cost = EOQ-based carrying + Safety Stock carrying + Ordering Cost $$TC = \sqrt{2DSH} + (\text{Safety Stock} \times H)$$ $$TC = 16{,}970.56 + 4{,}000$$ $$TC = ₹20{,}970.56 \text{ per year (variable cost portion)}$$ Adding purchase cost: $$TC_{total} = (24{,}000 \times 50) + 20{,}970.56 = ₹12{,}00{,}000 + 20{,}970.56 = ₹12{,}20{,}970.56$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing H:** Many students use the carrying cost *rate* (20%) instead of computing **H = rate × unit cost**. Always convert to absolute ₹/unit/year. > 2. **Forgetting Safety Stock Holding Cost:** Safety stock adds carrying cost but does **not** change EOQ. Students often wrongly recompute EOQ. > 3. **Wrong Reorder Point Formula:** Some use $R = L$ (just lead time, no demand). Correct form is $R = d \times L + \text{SS}$. > 4. **Missing "Number of Orders" is Fractional:** N rarely comes out as a whole number in real problems. Do not round N before computing total cost. > 5. **Not Equating Ordering = Carrying Cost:** KTU valuations award **2 marks** specifically for verifying this equality at EOQ. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [x] **EOQ Definition:** The order quantity that minimizes total variable inventory cost. - [x] **Master Formula:** $Q^* = \sqrt{2DS / H}$ — always with positive root. - [x] **Optimality Condition:** At $Q^*$, **Ordering Cost = Carrying Cost**. - [x] **Minimum TVC:** $TVC_{min} = \sqrt{2DSH}$. - [x] **Reorder Point:** $R = d \times L + \text{Safety Stock}$. - [x] **EOQ is independent of unit price P** (in the basic model without quantity discounts). - [x] **Safety stock does NOT change EOQ** — only changes reorder point and total holding cost. - [x] **Holding Cost H** is always in ₹/unit/year, never a percentage directly. - [x] **Cycle time** $T = 365 / N$ days. - [x] **Average Inventory** at EOQ = $Q^*/2$. - [x] **Assumption Checklist:** Constant D, no stockouts, instant replenishment, no discounts. - [x] **Agro-Specific:** Spoilage, seasonality, and perishability often require **modified EOQ (with safety stock / shortages)**. - [x] **KTU Trick:** If H doubles, $Q^*$ reduces by factor $\sqrt{2}$ ≈ 29.3%. - [x] **Validation Step:** Always confirm $\frac{D}{Q^*} \cdot S = \frac{Q^*}{2} \cdot H$ for full marks. ---

topics.title: ABC Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT865 - AGRO-INDUSTRIAL PROJECT PLANNING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** ABC Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ABC Analysis — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

**ABC Analysis** is an **inventory classification and management technique** based on the **Pareto Principle (80/20 Rule)**, in which a firm's inventory items are categorized into three distinct classes — **A, B, and C** — according to their **Annual Consumption Value (ACV)**. Items contributing the highest monetary consumption are placed under class A and receive the **tightest managerial control**, while low-value items under class C are managed with **relaxed procedures**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** ABC Analysis is a material management tool used to categorize inventory into three groups based on the relative significance of annual consumption value, enabling prioritization of managerial attention, capital, and storage effort.

## Intuitive Analogy — "The Wardrobe Rule"

Imagine you are packing a suitcase for a one-year trip:

- You carry **2 expensive leather jackets** (high value, low quantity) → you check them carefully, insure them, and protect them from rain. These are your **"A" items**.
- You carry **5 decent shirts** (medium value, medium quantity) → you hang them properly, but you are not paranoid about them. These are your **"B" items**.
- You carry **20 pairs of cheap socks** (low value, high quantity) → you just stuff them in a bag; losing one does not hurt. These are your **"C" items**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Core Insight:** Not all inventory items deserve equal attention. A small fraction of items typically consumes the lion's share of capital — this asymmetry is the foundation of ABC analysis.

## Key Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Annual Consumption Value (ACV):** **Annual demand × Unit cost** — the primary classification metric.
- **Cumulative Percentage:** Running total of sorted item values expressed as a percentage of the grand total.
- **Class A Items:** Typically **10–20%** of items consuming **70–80%** of total value.
- **Class B Items:** Typically **20–30%** of items consuming **15–25%** of total value.
- **Class C Items:** Typically **50–70%** of items consuming **5–10%** of total value.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Underlying Theoretical Logic

The ABC technique rests on three pillars of operational reasoning:

1. **Value Asymmetry:** In virtually every real-world inventory (spares, raw materials, finished goods), a small subset of items accounts for the majority of annual expenditure. This is the empirical **Pareto Distribution**.
2. **Differential Control Justification:** Tight control has an associated administrative cost. Applying rigorous control to every item is wasteful — it should be reserved for items where the **savings from preventing stockouts/overstocking exceed the cost of the control mechanism**.
3. **Managerial Attention Optimization:** By segregating items, the inventory manager can allocate **floor space, capital, safety stock, and order frequency** in proportion to monetary risk.

> [!NOTE]
> **Vital Few vs. Trivial Many:** A items are the "vital few" that drive the budget. C items are the "trivial many" that need only minimal supervision.

## Control Policies for Each Class

| Class | Inventory Policy | Order Frequency | Safety Stock | Record Keeping |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **A** | Rigorous, tight control | Frequent small orders | Low | Detailed, perpetual records |
| **B** | Moderate control | Periodic review | Moderate | Moderate tracking |
| **C** | Loose / simple control | Bulk orders, infrequent | High (bulk cushion) | Minimal, often bin-card only |

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Purpose / Application |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| **Annual Consumption Value (ACV)** | $\text{ACV}_i = D_i \times C_i$ | Converts physical quantity into monetary weight for ranking |
| **Percentage of Total Value** | $\%V_i = \dfrac{\text{ACV}_i}{\sum \text{ACV}} \times 100$ | Measures each item's share of total inventory cost |
| **Cumulative Percentage** | $\text{Cum}\%_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \%V_i$ | Determines the cut-off point for A, B, C classification |
| **Total Inventory Value** | $V_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} D_i \cdot C_i$ | Denominator for percentage calculations |
| **Number of Items in Class A** | $n_A \approx 10\% \text{ to } 20\% \text{ of } n$ | Planning input for control effort estimation |
| **Pareto Rule (Empirical)** | $V_A \approx 0.7 \text{ to } 0.8 \cdot V_{\text{total}}$ | Quick sanity check during board exam problems |

> Where $D_i$ = Annual demand of item $i$, $C_i$ = Unit cost of item $i$, $n$ = total number of distinct items.

## Real-World Utility in Agro-Industrial Engineering

- **Cold Storage Management:** Classify perishable vs. non-perishable raw materials — A items get climate-controlled zones.
- **Spare Parts Inventory (Dairy/Food Plants):** Centrifuge seals and homogenizer gaskets are A items; standard bolts are C items.
- **Procurement Budgeting:** Capital is reserved first for A items (e.g., cocoa, specialty chemicals), then B (sugar, salt), then C (cleaning consumables).
- **Warehouse Layout:** A items placed closest to dispatch bays to minimize handling time.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Procedure for Conducting ABC Analysis

The standard six-step procedure applied universally in KTU problems is:

1. **Enumerate** all distinct inventory items (e.g., $i = 1, 2, \dots, n$).
2. **Compute** the Annual Consumption Value $\text{ACV}_i = D_i \times C_i$ for every item.
3. **Rank** items in **descending order** of ACV.
4. **Calculate** $\%V_i$ and the running **Cumulative Percentage** $\text{Cum}\%_i$.
5. **Apply** cut-off rules: typically $\text{Cum}\% \le 70\% \to$ Class A, $70\% < \text{Cum}\% \le 90\% \to$ Class B, $\text{Cum}\% > 90\% \to$ Class C.
6. **Decide** the appropriate control policy for each class.

## Worked Numerical Example (Agro-Industrial Context)

**Scenario:** A fruit-processing plant maintains inventory of 8 raw material and consumable items. The annual demand and unit cost data are tabulated below. Apply ABC analysis.

| Item Code | Material | Annual Demand $D_i$ | Unit Cost $C_i$ (₹) |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|
| RM1 | Mango Pulp Concentrate | 2,000 kg | 250 |
| RM2 | Sugar | 10,000 kg | 40 |
| RM3 | Citric Acid | 500 kg | 300 |
| RM4 | Pectin | 400 kg | 600 |
| RM5 | Glass Bottles | 50,000 units | 8 |
| RM6 | Carton Boxes | 20,000 units | 15 |
| RM7 | Sodium Benzoate | 100 kg | 200 |
| RM8 | Cleaning Detergent | 800 L | 50 |

### Step 1 — Compute Annual Consumption Value (ACV)

Applying $\text{ACV}_i = D_i \times C_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ACV}_{\text{RM1}} &= 2000 \times 250 = 5{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹} \\
\text{ACV}_{\text{RM2}} &= 10000 \times 40 = 4{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹} \\
\text{ACV}_{\text{RM3}} &= 500 \times 300 = 1{,}50{,}000 \text{ ₹} \\
\text{ACV}_{\text{RM4}} &= 400 \times 600 = 2{,}40{,}000 \text{ ₹} \\
\text{ACV}_{\text{RM5}} &= 50000 \times 8 = 4{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹} \\
\text{ACV}_{\text{RM6}} &= 20000 \times 15 = 3{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹} \\
\text{ACV}_{\text{RM7}} &= 100 \times 200 = 20{,}000 \text{ ₹} \\
\text{ACV}_{\text{RM8}} &= 800 \times 50 = 40{,}000 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Grand Total

$$
V_{\text{total}} = \sum \text{ACV}_i = 5{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 + 2{,}40{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 + 20{,}000 + 40{,}000
$$

$$
V_{\text{total}} = 20{,}50{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

### Step 3 — Sort in Descending Order of ACV

| Rank | Item | ACV (₹) | $\%V_i$ | Cumulative (₹) | $\text{Cum}\%_i$ |
|:---:|:---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 1 | RM1 Mango Pulp | 5,00,000 | 24.39% | 5,00,000 | 24.39% |
| 2 | RM2 Sugar | 4,00,000 | 19.51% | 9,00,000 | 43.90% |
| 3 | RM5 Glass Bottles | 4,00,000 | 19.51% | 13,00,000 | 63.41% |
| 4 | RM6 Carton Boxes | 3,00,000 | 14.63% | 16,00,000 | 78.05% |
| 5 | RM4 Pectin | 2,40,000 | 11.71% | 18,40,000 | 89.76% |
| 6 | RM3 Citric Acid | 1,50,000 | 7.32% | 19,90,000 | 97.07% |
| 7 | RM8 Detergent | 40,000 | 1.95% | 20,30,000 | 99.02% |
| 8 | RM7 Sodium Benzoate | 20,000 | 0.98% | 20,50,000 | 100.00% |

**Sample percentage calculations shown explicitly:**

$$
\%V_{\text{RM1}} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{20{,}50{,}000} \times 100 = 24.39\%
$$

$$
\text{Cum}\%_{\text{RM3}} = 24.39 + 19.51 + 19.51 = 63.41\%
$$

### Step 4 — Apply Classification Cut-offs

| Class | Cut-off Rule | Items Included | Count | Value Share |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|---:|
| **A** | $\text{Cum}\% \le 70\%$ | RM1, RM2, RM5, RM6 | 4 (50%) | 78.05% |
| **B** | $70\% < \text{Cum}\% \le 90\%$ | RM4 | 1 (12.5%) | 11.71% |
| **C** | $\text{Cum}\% > 90\%$ | RM3, RM8, RM7 | 3 (37.5%) | 10.24% |

### Interpretation of Results

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Even though 4 of 8 items fall into Class A in this dataset, the principle is preserved — a small number of items (50%) consume the bulk of working capital (78%). Notice that **Glass Bottles** rank alongside **Sugar** despite low unit cost, purely because of massive volume — a classic illustration of why ABC uses **monetary value, not quantity**.

### Cross-Verification (Pareto Check)

$$
V_A / V_{\text{total}} = \frac{16{,}00{,}000}{20{,}50{,}000} = 78.05\% \quad \checkmark \text{ (within 70–80% expected range)}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Figure 1 — Process Flow of ABC Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Enumerate all inventory items] --> B[Step 1: Collect annual demand Di and unit cost Ci]
    B --> C[Step 2: Compute ACV = Di x Ci for each item]
    C --> D[Step 3: Rank items in descending order of ACV]
    D --> E[Step 4: Compute total value V total]
    E --> F[Step 5: Calculate percent value and cumulative percent]
    F --> G{Step 6: Apply cut-off thresholds}
    G -->|Cum percent less than or equal to 70 percent| H[Class A: Tight control, frequent review]
    G -->|Cum percent between 70 and 90 percent| I[Class B: Moderate control, periodic review]
    G -->|Cum percent greater than 90 percent| J[Class C: Loose control, bulk ordering]
    H --> K[End: Assign storage, safety stock and order policy]
    I --> K
    J --> K
```

## Figure 2 — Functional Architecture of the ABC Classification Block

```mermaid
graph LR
    INPUT[Raw Input: Inventory Master File] --> PROC[Processing Engine: ACV Computation and Ranking]
    PROC --> SORT[Sorted Vector by Monetary Value]
    SORT --> CLAS[Classifier Module: Threshold Logic]
    CLAS --> OUTA[Output Stream A: Critical Items]
    CLAS --> OUTB[Output Stream B: Moderate Items]
    CLAS --> OUTC[Output Stream C: Low-Value Items]
    OUTA --> DECA[Decision Layer: Tight Control and High Attention]
    OUTB --> DECB[Decision Layer: Moderate Attention and Periodic Review]
    OUTC --> DECC[Decision Layer: Bulk Stocking and Minimal Records]
```

## Figure 3 — Cumulative Value Curve (Pareto Curve) — Schematic Description

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Interpretation:** Plot the sorted items on the X-axis (Item Rank 1 to $n$) and the Cumulative Percentage on the Y-axis. The resulting curve rises **steeply** for the first few items (Class A region), **moderately** through Class B, and **flattens** sharply for Class C. The "knee" of the curve visually marks the A–B boundary; the "elbow" marks the B–C boundary.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1.** Define ABC analysis. State the percentage distribution of items and value for each class.

**Model Answer:**
ABC analysis is an inventory classification technique based on the Pareto principle that categorizes stock items into three groups — A, B, and C — according to their annual consumption value.

- **Class A:** ~10–20% of items consuming **70–80%** of value.
- **Class B:** ~20–30% of items consuming **15–25%** of value.
- **Class C:** ~50–70% of items consuming **5–10%** of value.

Class A items receive the tightest managerial control, while Class C items are managed with the simplest procedures.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q2.** List any three differences between the control policies applicable to Class A and Class C items.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Class A Items | Class C Items |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Order Frequency | Frequent, smaller orders | Infrequent, bulk orders |
| Safety Stock | Maintained low | Maintained high (cushion stock) |
| Record Keeping | Detailed, perpetual inventory records | Minimal, often bin-card only |
| Forecasting Method | Sophisticated statistical models | Simple rule-of-thumb estimates |

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **steps involved in conducting ABC analysis** with the help of a neat flowchart. Mention the rationale behind each step.

**(b)** [7 Marks] An agro-warehouse has 6 inventory items with the following data:

| Item | Annual Usage (units) | Unit Cost (₹) |
|:---:|---:|---:|
| P1 | 4,000 | 150 |
| P2 | 1,000 | 80 |
| P3 | 600 | 500 |
| P4 | 3,000 | 40 |
| P5 | 500 | 250 |
| P6 | 2,000 | 60 |

Classify the items into A, B, and C categories using ABC analysis. State the **control policy** you would recommend for Class A items.

### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Steps in ABC Analysis:**

1. **List all inventory items** — Enumerate every distinct SKU/material to be classified. *Rationale:* Ensures complete coverage; missing an item defeats the analysis.
2. **Determine Annual Demand and Unit Cost** — Gather usage and price data for one full year. *Rationale:* Both data are required to compute monetary weight.
3. **Compute Annual Consumption Value** — $\text{ACV}_i = D_i \times C_i$. *Rationale:* Converts physical quantity into a common monetary scale.
4. **Rank Items in Descending Order of ACV** — Highest value first. *Rationale:* Enables identification of the "vital few."
5. **Compute Percentage Value and Cumulative Percentage** — Allows threshold-based classification. *Rationale:* The cumulative view reveals the Pareto structure.
6. **Classify into A, B, C using standard cut-offs** (≤70%, 70–90%, >90%). *Rationale:* Translates numbers into actionable categories.
7. **Recommend Control Policy** for each class.

*(Flowchart: Refer to Section 4, Figure 1.)*

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute ACV:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ACV}_{P1} &= 4000 \times 150 = 6{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{ACV}_{P2} &= 1000 \times 80 = 80{,}000 \\
\text{ACV}_{P3} &= 600 \times 500 = 3{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{ACV}_{P4} &= 3000 \times 40 = 1{,}20{,}000 \\
\text{ACV}_{P5} &= 500 \times 250 = 1{,}25{,}000 \\
\text{ACV}_{P6} &= 2000 \times 60 = 1{,}20{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2 — Total value:

$$
V_{\text{total}} = 6{,}00{,}000 + 80{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}000 + 1{,}25{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}000 = 13{,}45{,}000
$$

Step 3 — Sort and tabulate:

| Rank | Item | ACV (₹) | $\%V_i$ | $\text{Cum}\%$ | Class |
|:---:|:---:|---:|---:|---:|:---:|
| 1 | P1 | 6,00,000 | 44.61% | 44.61% | A |
| 2 | P3 | 3,00,000 | 22.30% | 66.91% | A |
| 3 | P5 | 1,25,000 | 9.29% | 76.20% | B |
| 4 | P4 | 1,20,000 | 8.92% | 85.13% | B |
| 5 | P6 | 1,20,000 | 8.92% | 94.05% | C |
| 6 | P2 | 80,000 | 5.95% | 100.00% | C |

**Result:**
- **Class A:** P1, P3 (33% of items, 66.91% of value)
- **Class B:** P5, P4 (33% of items, 18.21% of value)
- **Class C:** P6, P2 (33% of items, 14.87% of value)

**Control Policy for Class A Items (P1, P3):**
- Frequent, small-quantity orders to minimize carrying cost.
- **Low safety stock** — use Just-In-Time (JIT) philosophy.
- **Tight perpetual inventory records** updated in real time.
- **Rigorous forecasting** using statistical/Economic Order Quantity models.
- **Senior management attention** and physical security.
- **Close vendor coordination** to prevent stockouts.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** [7 Marks] State and explain the **Pareto Principle** as it applies to inventory management. How does it justify the existence of differential control policies?

**(b)** [7 Marks] A spice-milling unit has 7 items of inventory. Classify them using ABC analysis and comment on the managerial implications.

| Item | Annual Demand (kg) | Unit Cost (₹/kg) |
|:---:|---:|---:|
| S1 | 5,000 | 80 |
| S2 | 200 | 1,200 |
| S3 | 800 | 250 |
| S4 | 1,500 | 60 |
| S5 | 100 | 800 |
| S6 | 600 | 400 |
| S7 | 300 | 150 |

### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Pareto Principle:**
The Pareto Principle (also called the **80/20 Rule**, named after Italian economist Vilfredo Pareto) states that for many phenomena, **roughly 80% of the effects come from 20% of the causes**.

Applied to inventory: **~80% of the annual consumption value is contributed by ~20% of the items**.

**Justification for Differential Control:**
- Rigorous control systems (e.g., perpetual inventory, EOQ models) carry an **administrative cost** in terms of staff time, software, and management attention.
- Applying such intensive systems to all $n$ items would impose a cost greater than the value saved.
- Therefore, management should invest the **most rigorous control on the vital few (Class A)** and the **simplest control on the trivial many (Class C)** — achieving **optimal cost-to-control balance**.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute ACV:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ACV}_{S1} &= 5000 \times 80 = 4{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{ACV}_{S2} &= 200 \times 1200 = 2{,}40{,}000 \\
\text{ACV}_{S3} &= 800 \times 250 = 2{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{ACV}_{S4} &= 1500 \times 60 = 90{,}000 \\
\text{ACV}_{S5} &= 100 \times 800 = 80{,}000 \\
\text{ACV}_{S6} &= 600 \times 400 = 2{,}40{,}000 \\
\text{ACV}_{S7} &= 300 \times 150 = 45{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2 — Total value:

$$
V_{\text{total}} = 4{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}40{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 + 90{,}000 + 80{,}000 + 2{,}40{,}000 + 45{,}000 = 12{,}95{,}000
$$

Step 3 — Sort descending:

| Rank | Item | ACV (₹) | $\%V_i$ | $\text{Cum}\%$ | Class |
|:---:|:---:|---:|---:|---:|:---:|
| 1 | S1 | 4,00,000 | 30.89% | 30.89% | A |
| 2 | S2 | 2,40,000 | 18.53% | 49.42% | A |
| 3 | S6 | 2,40,000 | 18.53% | 67.95% | A |
| 4 | S3 | 2,00,000 | 15.44% | 83.40% | B |
| 5 | S4 | 90,000 | 6.95% | 90.35% | B |
| 6 | S5 | 80,000 | 6.18% | 96.53% | C |
| 7 | S7 | 45,000 | 3.47% | 100.00% | C |

**Result:**
- **Class A:** S1, S2, S6 → 43% items, **67.95%** value
- **Class B:** S3, S4 → 29% items, **22.39%** value
- **Class C:** S5, S7 → 29% items, **9.65%** value

**Managerial Implications:**
- The mill should **prioritize procurement capital** for S1 (bulk spice, e.g., turmeric), S2 (high-cost saffron-grade spice), and S6.
- S5 and S7 can be ordered in **large bulk quantities** with relaxed stock monitoring.
- The unit should adopt a **vendor-managed inventory (VMI)** arrangement for S2, given its high unit cost and value density.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Deductions**
> 1. **Wrong sort order:** Sorting items in *ascending* order of ACV before computing cumulative percentage — **loses 2–3 marks** immediately.
> 2. **Forgetting to compute $\%V_i$ and $\text{Cum}\%$:** Many students directly guess A, B, C without tabular justification — **partial marking denied**.
> 3. **Stating cut-offs without showing the computation:** Always tabulate. The step-by-step table is the single largest mark-earner in the 7-mark numerical sub-question.
> 4. **Unit mismatch:** Annual demand may be in kg/L/units and unit cost in ₹/kg, etc. — always cross-check units; mismatched units yield zero credit for ACV.
> 5. **Confusing ABC with VED, SDE, or FSN analysis:** These are different classification systems (used in spare parts / pharmacy contexts). ABC is purely **monetary-value-based**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** ABC analysis classifies inventory by **Annual Consumption Value** into A (high value), B (moderate), C (low).
- **Core formula:** $\text{ACV} = D \times C$; $\%V = \dfrac{\text{ACV}_i}{V_{\text{total}}} \times 100$.
- **Standard cut-offs:** A ≤ 70%, B = 70–90%, C > 90% of cumulative value.
- **Approximate distribution:** 10–20% items → 70–80% value (A); 50–70% items → 5–10% value (C).
- **Foundation:** Built on the **Pareto Principle (80/20 Rule)**.
- **Control philosophy:** Tight control on A, moderate on B, loose on C.
- **Procedure:** Enumerate → Compute ACV → Sort descending → Compute %V and Cum% → Classify → Apply policy.
- **Agro-industrial relevance:** Spare parts of food processing equipment, raw material inventory, packaging material prioritization.
- **Pitfall to avoid:** Classification is based on **monetary value**, not physical quantity — high-volume low-cost items can still be A items.
- **Always present a sorted table** with item code, ACV, %V, and Cum% columns in your answer script.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Financial Management - Assessing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT865 - AGRO-INDUSTRIAL PROJECT PLANNING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Financial Management - Assessing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Financial Management – Assessing** in agro-industrial project planning refers to the **systematic evaluation, measurement, and appraisal of the financial viability, profitability, liquidity, and risk** of an agricultural or food-processing enterprise across its project life cycle. It encompasses **capital budgeting techniques, working capital assessment, cost–benefit analysis, break-even analysis, ratio analysis, and risk-adjusted appraisal** to determine whether a proposed agro-industrial project (e.g., a rice mill, dairy plant, cold storage, or food processing unit) is financially sound and worth the capital commitment.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** framework, financial assessment is one of the most heavily tested components of Module 2 because it integrates engineering project economics with agro-business decision-making. The key objectives are:

- Determine whether a project will **recover its capital outlay** within an acceptable horizon.
- Quantify **profitability** through Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) measures.
- Assess **financial risk** and **liquidity** under seasonal variability typical of agri-business.
- Provide an objective basis for **Go / No-Go investment decisions**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a farmer wants to start a small **turmeric powder processing unit** with an investment of ₹20 Lakhs. Before he commits, he must *assess* — i.e., answer three plain questions:
> 1. **How long will it take to get my ₹20 Lakhs back?** *(Payback Period)*
> 2. **After accounting for the time-value of money, will this unit make me richer or poorer?** *(Net Present Value)*
> 3. **What is the worst level of sales at which I neither profit nor lose?** *(Break-Even Point)*
>
> Financial assessment is the discipline that converts these intuitive questions into **rigorous numerical answers** using standardized techniques, ensuring the agro-industrial decision is **objective, defensible, and bankable**.

### 1.3 Standard Financial Metrics in Agro-Industrial Assessment

- **Cost of Capital (r)** — typically **8%–15%** for Indian agro-industries (subsidized) and **12%–18%** for private commercial lending.
- **Project Life (n)** — usually **10–15 years** for agro-processing plants; **20–25 years** for plantations / orchards.
- **Discount Rate** — often the **weighted average cost of capital (WACC)** or the bank lending rate + risk premium.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Building Blocks of Financial Assessment

Financial assessment of agro-industrial projects is built on **three pillars**:

1. **Cash Flow Estimation** — accurate projection of inflows (revenue from sale of processed product) and outflows (raw material, labor, utilities, depreciation, taxes).
2. **Time Value of Money (TVM)** — a rupee today is worth more than the same rupee next year because of inflation, opportunity cost, and risk.
3. **Risk Adjustment** — agro-businesses face **climatic risk**, **price volatility**, **seasonal raw-material supply**, and **perishability**, all of which must be quantified via sensitivity analysis and scenario testing.

### 2.2 Why and How Each Step Works

- **Cash Flow Estimation** is *necessary* because accounting profit ignores the actual timing of money movement; only cash flows can fund debt repayment in an agro-unit.
- **TVM Discounting** is applied because agribusiness cycles are long (sugarcane → 1 year; dairy daily; orchard 5–7 years before fruiting).
- **Risk Adjustment** is built into the discount rate or simulated via separate scenarios — critical for KTU exam answers because examiners reward "what-if" thinking.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **master reference** for solving any KTU 2024 question on this topic. Memorize the formulas and their units.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Simple Payback Period | $PP = \dfrac{I_0}{A}$ | years; $I_0$ = Initial Investment, $A$ = constant Annual Cash Inflow |
| 2 | Discounted Payback Period | $PP_d$ = first $t$ where $\sum_{t=1}^{T}\dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} \geq I_0$ | years; uses DCF |
| 3 | Net Present Value (NPV) | $NPV = \displaystyle\sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | ₹ Lakhs; **Accept if NPV $\geq$ 0** |
| 4 | Internal Rate of Return (IRR) | $NPV(r) = 0$, solve for $r$ | **%**; **Accept if IRR $\geq$ cost of capital** |
| 5 | Benefit–Cost Ratio (BCR) | $BCR = \dfrac{\sum \dfrac{B_t}{(1+r)^t}}{\sum \dfrac{C_t}{(1+r)^t}}$ | dimensionless; **Accept if BCR $\geq$ 1** |
| 6 | Profitability Index (PI) | $PI = \dfrac{PV\ of\ future\ CFs}{I_0}$ | dimensionless; **Accept if PI $\geq$ 1** |
| 7 | Break-Even Quantity | $Q^* = \dfrac{FC}{p - v}$ | units / tonnes; $FC$ = Fixed Cost, $p$ = price/unit, $v$ = variable cost/unit |
| 8 | Break-Even Sales (₹) | $S^* = \dfrac{FC}{1 - \dfrac{VC}{S}}$ | ₹ Lakhs; uses contribution margin |
| 9 | Return on Investment (ROI) | $ROI = \dfrac{Net\ Profit}{Investment} \times 100$ | **%** |
| 10 | Net Present Worth (Agro cost-benefit) | $NPW = \displaystyle\sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{B_t - C_t}{(1+i)^t}$ | ₹ Lakhs |
| 11 | Annuity Equivalent | $AE = \dfrac{NPV \cdot r}{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}$ | ₹ Lakhs / year |
| 12 | Sensitivity % | $\Delta\% = \dfrac{\vert X_{new} - X_{base} \vert}{\vert X_{base} \vert} \times 100$ | **%** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention note for KTU exam scripts:** Always state the **decision rule** explicitly after computing any metric. For example: *"Since NPV = ₹12.4 Lakhs > 0, the project is financially viable and should be accepted."* Examiners award full marks **only** when both the calculation and the decision rule are shown.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

These financial metrics are used by **NABARD, RBI, commercial banks, and agri-finance corporations** to evaluate loan proposals for cold chains, food parks, dairy cooperatives, sugar mills, and oil extraction units. Mastery of these techniques is mandatory for agro-industrial project managers, agri-preneurs, and rural development officers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Present Value (PV) Factor

The **Present Value (PV)** of a future cash flow $CF_t$ received at the end of year $t$ is the amount of money today that, if invested at discount rate $r$, would grow to $CF_t$.

$$
\text{Future Value after } t \text{ years} = PV \cdot (1 + r)^t
$$

Equating future value to the cash flow:

$$
CF_t = PV \cdot (1 + r)^t
$$

Solving for $PV$:

$$
PV = \dfrac{CF_t}{(1 + r)^t}
$$

**Interpretation:** $1/(1+r)^t$ is the **discount factor**, which shrinks future cash to today's value. As $t$ increases or $r$ increases, $PV$ decreases — reflecting the time-value of money.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Break-Even Quantity

At break-even, **Total Revenue = Total Cost**.

$$
\text{Total Cost} = FC + v \cdot Q
$$

$$
\text{Total Revenue} = p \cdot Q
$$

Setting them equal:

$$
p \cdot Q^* = FC + v \cdot Q^*
$$

$$
(p - v) \cdot Q^* = FC
$$

$$
Q^* = \dfrac{FC}{p - v}
$$

**Interpretation for an agro-unit:** If a small **oil mill** has fixed cost = ₹3,00,000/year, selling price = ₹150/litre, variable cost = ₹90/litre, then break-even = 3,00,000 / (150 − 90) = **5,000 litres/year**. The unit must sell *at least* this much to avoid loss.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Equivalent Uniform Annual Cost / Annuity

If a project has NPV at the end of year $n$, its **annuity equivalent** (AE) is the constant annual amount over $n$ years whose PV equals NPV.

$$
NPV = AE \cdot \left[\dfrac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}\right]
$$

Solving for $AE$:

$$
AE = NPV \cdot \dfrac{r}{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}
$$

**Use in agro-projects:** Useful when comparing projects of unequal life — e.g., a **5-year dairy project** vs. a **10-year food-grain silo**.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example: Full NPV Assessment of a Cold Storage Unit

**Project Data:**
- Initial Investment $I_0$ = ₹50,00,000
- Annual Cash Inflow $CF$ = ₹12,00,000 (for 6 years, end-of-year)
- Discount Rate $r$ = 12%
- Salvage Value (end of year 6) = ₹5,00,000

**Step 1: Compute PV of each year's cash flow.**

$$
PV_1 = \dfrac{12{,}00{,}000}{(1.12)^1} = 10{,}71{,}428.57
$$

$$
PV_2 = \dfrac{12{,}00{,}000}{(1.12)^2} = 9{,}56{,}632.65
$$

$$
PV_3 = \dfrac{12{,}00{,}000}{(1.12)^3} = 8{,}54{,}136.30
$$

$$
PV_4 = \dfrac{12{,}00{,}000}{(1.12)^4} = 7{,}63{,}514.55
$$

$$
PV_5 = \dfrac{12{,}00{,}000}{(1.12)^5} = 6{,}81{,}709.42
$$

$$
PV_6 = \dfrac{12{,}00{,}000}{(1.12)^6} = 6{,}08{,}669.12
$$

**Step 2: Add PV of Salvage Value in year 6.**

$$
PV_{SV} = \dfrac{5{,}00{,}000}{(1.12)^6} = 2{,}53{,}612.13
$$

**Step 3: Sum all PVs.**

$$
\sum PV = 10{,}71{,}428.57 + 9{,}56{,}632.65 + 8{,}54{,}136.30 + 7{,}63{,}514.55 + 6{,}81{,}709.42 + 6{,}08{,}669.12 + 2{,}53{,}612.13
$$

$$
\sum PV = 51{,}89{,}702.74
$$

**Step 4: Compute NPV.**

$$
NPV = \sum PV - I_0 = 51{,}89{,}702.74 - 50{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}89{,}702.74
$$

$$
\boxed{NPV \approx \text{₹}\,1{,}89{,}703 > 0 \ \Rightarrow \text{Accept the cold storage project.}}
$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix

| Metric | Computed Value | Decision Rule | Project Verdict |
|--------|---------------|---------------|-----------------|
| NPV | ₹ +1,89,703 | NPV ≥ 0 | **Accept** |
| BCR | 51.90 / 50.00 = **1.038** | BCR ≥ 1 | **Accept** |
| ROI (avg.) | 1,89,703 / 50,00,000 ≈ **3.79%** over 6 yrs | context-specific | Marginal — needs sensitivity check |
| Payback | 50,00,000 / 12,00,000 ≈ **4.17 yrs** | < project life | **Accept** |

---

### 3.6 IRR Approximation via Linear Interpolation (When Direct Solve is Hard)

Use when trial-and-error is required:

$$
IRR \approx r_L + \dfrac{NPV_L}{NPV_L - NPV_H} \cdot (r_H - r_L)
$$

Where:
- $r_L$ = lower discount rate (NPV at this rate = $NPV_L$ > 0)
- $r_H$ = higher discount rate (NPV at this rate = $NPV_H$ < 0)

**Example:** NPV at 12% = +1.90 L; NPV at 18% = −3.10 L.

$$
IRR \approx 12\% + \dfrac{1.90}{1.90 - (-3.10)} \cdot (18\% - 12\%)
$$

$$
IRR \approx 12\% + \dfrac{1.90}{5.00} \cdot 6\% = 12\% + 2.28\% = 14.28\%
$$

**Decision:** If cost of capital = 12%, then IRR = 14.28% > 12%, **Accept**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Financial Assessment Workflow (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Project Idea Agro Industrial] --> A2[Estimate Cash Flows Inflows and Outflows]
    A2 --> A3[Choose Discount Rate WACC or Bank Rate]
    A3 --> A4{Apply Financial Metrics}
    A4 --> A41[NPV Computation]
    A4 --> A42[IRR Computation]
    A4 --> A43[BCR Computation]
    A4 --> A44[Payback Period]
    A4 --> A45[Break Even Analysis]
    A41 --> A5[Apply Decision Rules]
    A42 --> A5
    A43 --> A5
    A44 --> A5
    A45 --> A5
    A5 --> A6{Viable Project}
    A6 --> A61[Yes Proceed to Financing]
    A6 --> A62[No Reject or Redesign]
    A61 --> A7[Risk and Sensitivity Check]
    A62 --> A8[Project Closure]
    A7 --> A9[Final Investment Decision]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram of Financial Assessment Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Inputs Investment Cost] --> C1[Financial Assessment Engine]
    B2[Inputs Revenue Forecast] --> C1
    B3[Inputs Operating Costs] --> C1
    B4[Inputs Discount Rate] --> C1
    B5[Inputs Project Life] --> C1
    C1 --> D1[Time Value of Money Module]
    C1 --> D2[Risk Adjustment Module]
    C1 --> D3[Ratio Analysis Module]
    D1 --> E1[Output NPV]
    D2 --> E2[Output Sensitivity Matrix]
    D3 --> E3[Output BCR ROI IRR]
    E1 --> F1[Investment Decision Layer]
    E2 --> F1
    E3 --> F1
    F1 --> G1[Final Go No Go Verdict]
```

### 4.3 Break-Even Visualization Logic (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Start] --> P2[Fixed Cost FC]
    P2 --> P3[Variable Cost per Unit v]
    P3 --> P4[Selling Price per Unit p]
    P4 --> P5{Compute Contribution p minus v}
    P5 --> P6[Compute Break Even Quantity Q star = FC divided by p minus v]
    P6 --> P7{Q star versus Actual Forecast Sales}
    P7 --> P8[Forecast greater than Q star Profitable]
    P7 --> P9[Forecast less than Q star Loss Zone]
    P8 --> P10[Accept Project]
    P9 --> P11[Reject or Restructure]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation for KTU Exam Scripts:** When asked to "draw the financial appraisal framework" in a 14-mark question, reproducing the structure above (or any equivalent hierarchical block diagram) earns **2–3 marks** as a visual aid. Always label each block with its full technical term — partial labels lose marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

Define **Net Present Value (NPV)**. State its decision rule.

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Net Present Value (NPV)** is the algebraic sum of the present values of all cash inflows and outflows associated with a project, discounted at the appropriate cost of capital over its entire economic life.

$$
NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1 + r)^t}
$$

**Decision Rule:** *A project is financially acceptable if and only if NPV ≥ 0.* A positive NPV means the project is expected to generate value above the minimum required rate of return; a negative NPV means it will destroy value and must be rejected. **(3 Marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

What is the **Break-Even Point (BEP)** in the context of an agro-processing unit? Give the formula for BEP in units.

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> The **Break-Even Point (BEP)** is the level of production or sales at which **Total Revenue equals Total Cost**, so the agro-unit makes **neither profit nor loss**. It is a critical financial indicator because it reveals the minimum output required to survive financially, especially in seasonal agri-businesses.

**Formula for BEP in units:**

$$
Q^* = \dfrac{FC}{p - v}
$$

Where $FC$ = Total Fixed Cost, $p$ = Selling Price per unit, $v$ = Variable Cost per unit. **(3 Marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]*

**(a)** A proposed agro-industrial project requires an initial investment of **₹40 Lakhs**. It is expected to generate net cash inflows of **₹10 Lakhs per annum for 7 years**. The cost of capital is **10%**. Compute the **NPV** and **Profitability Index (PI)**. Advise whether the project should be accepted. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain **IRR** and compute it approximately using linear interpolation for the same project, given that NPV at **15% discount rate** is **−₹1.20 Lakhs** (and NPV at 10% as computed above). Comment on its acceptability. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — NPV and PI

**Given:** $I_0$ = ₹40,00,000; $CF$ = ₹10,00,000/yr for 7 yrs; $r$ = 10%.

**Step 1: Compute the annuity factor (PVIFA) for 7 years at 10%.**

$$
PVIFA(10\%, 7) = \dfrac{1 - (1.10)^{-7}}{0.10}
$$

$$
(1.10)^{7} = 1.9487
$$

$$
PVIFA = \dfrac{1 - 0.5132}{0.10} = \dfrac{0.4868}{0.10} = 4.868
$$

**Step 2: PV of all cash inflows.**

$$
PV_{\text{inflows}} = 10{,}00{,}000 \times 4.868 = 48{,}68{,}000
$$

**Step 3: NPV.**

$$
NPV = 48{,}68{,}000 - 40{,}00{,}000 = 8{,}68{,}000
$$

$$
\boxed{NPV = \text{₹}\,8.68\ \text{Lakhs}}
$$

**Step 4: Profitability Index.**

$$
PI = \dfrac{PV_{\text{inflows}}}{I_0} = \dfrac{48{,}68{,}000}{40{,}00{,}000} = 1.217
$$

$$
\boxed{PI = 1.217}
$$

**Decision:** Since **NPV = +₹8.68 Lakhs > 0** and **PI = 1.217 > 1**, the project is **financially viable and should be accepted**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Part (b) — IRR via Linear Interpolation

**Definition:** Internal Rate of Return is the discount rate that makes the NPV of a project **exactly equal to zero**. It represents the **break-even rate of return** on the invested capital.

**Given data:**
- $r_L$ = 10%, $NPV_L$ = +₹8.68 Lakhs
- $r_H$ = 15%, $NPV_H$ = −₹1.20 Lakhs

**Step 1: Apply linear interpolation formula.**

$$
IRR \approx r_L + \left[\dfrac{NPV_L}{NPV_L - NPV_H}\right] \times (r_H - r_L)
$$

**Step 2: Substitute values.**

$$
IRR \approx 10\% + \left[\dfrac{8.68}{8.68 - (-1.20)}\right] \times (15\% - 10\%)
$$

$$
IRR \approx 10\% + \left[\dfrac{8.68}{9.88}\right] \times 5\%
$$

$$
IRR \approx 10\% + 0.8785 \times 5\%
$$

$$
IRR \approx 10\% + 4.39\%
$$

$$
\boxed{IRR \approx 14.39\%}
$$

**Decision Rule:** Accept the project if **IRR ≥ Cost of Capital (10%)**.

**Conclusion:** IRR ≈ **14.39% > 10%**, therefore the project is **acceptable on the IRR criterion as well**, confirming the NPV-based decision. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]*

**(a)** Discuss the concept of **Break-Even Analysis** in agro-industrial projects. A small-scale **dal (lentil) mill** has fixed costs of **₹2,40,000 per annum**, variable cost of **₹35 per kg**, and selling price of **₹55 per kg**. Compute the **Break-Even Quantity** and **Break-Even Sales (₹)**. If annual capacity is **20,000 kg**, what is the **Margin of Safety**? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain **sensitivity analysis** as a tool for financial assessment. If a 10% rise in raw-material cost reduces NPV from ₹8.68 Lakhs to ₹5.50 Lakhs, compute the **sensitivity percentage** and comment. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Break-Even Analysis

**Concept:** Break-Even Analysis identifies the production level at which the agro-unit neither earns profit nor incurs loss. It helps entrepreneurs understand the **minimum sales volume required to survive financially** — critical for seasonal businesses like dal milling.

**Given:** $FC$ = ₹2,40,000; $v$ = ₹35/kg; $p$ = ₹55/kg.

**Step 1: Compute contribution per kg.**

$$
\text{Contribution} = p - v = 55 - 35 = \text{₹}\,20\ \text{per kg}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Break-Even Quantity.**

$$
Q^* = \dfrac{FC}{p - v} = \dfrac{2{,}40{,}000}{20} = 12{,}000\ \text{kg}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q^* = 12{,}000\ \text{kg/yr}}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Break-Even Sales (in ₹).**

$$
S^* = Q^* \times p = 12{,}000 \times 55 = \text{₹}\,6{,}60{,}000
$$

**Step 4: Compute Margin of Safety (MoS) in kg and %.**

$$
MoS_{\text{kg}} = 20{,}000 - 12{,}000 = 8{,}000\ \text{kg}
$$

$$
MoS_{\%} = \dfrac{8{,}000}{20{,}000} \times 100 = 40\%
$$

**Interpretation:** The mill has a **40% safety buffer** — sales can fall by up to 40% before reaching break-even. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Part (b) — Sensitivity Analysis

**Concept:** Sensitivity Analysis measures how the **output financial metric (e.g., NPV) responds to changes in key input variables** (raw material cost, selling price, discount rate). It is the most common **risk-assessment tool** in agro-industrial project appraisal because it identifies the variables whose estimation errors cause the biggest financial damage.

**Given:** Base NPV = ₹8.68 Lakhs; New NPV (after +10% raw-material cost) = ₹5.50 Lakhs.

**Step 1: Compute absolute change.**

$$
\Delta NPV = 8.68 - 5.50 = \text{₹}\,3.18\ \text{Lakhs}
$$

**Step 2: Compute sensitivity percentage.**

$$
Sensitivity\ \% = \dfrac{\vert \Delta NPV \vert}{\vert NPV_{base} \vert} \times 100 = \dfrac{3.18}{8.68} \times 100 = 36.64\%
$$

$$
\boxed{Sensitivity \approx 36.64\%}
$$

**Step 3: Compute sensitivity ratio.**

$$
\text{Sensitivity Ratio} = \dfrac{\%\ \text{change in NPV}}{\%\ \text{change in input}} = \dfrac{36.64}{10} = 3.66
$$

**Comment:** A **36.64% drop in NPV for a 10% rise in raw-material cost** indicates that **raw-material cost is a highly sensitive variable** (sensitivity ratio > 1). The mill should **hedge raw-material procurement** through contracts, futures, or cooperative sourcing. The project is still viable (NPV > 0), but risk mitigation is essential. **(7 Marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Financial Assessment Questions:**
>
> 1. **Omitting the decision rule** — Students compute NPV correctly but forget to write *"Accept/Reject because NPV ≥ 0."* Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** specifically for missing this. **Always end with a verdict.**
> 2. **Using wrong discount rate** — In NPV/IRR problems, students sometimes use the cash inflow as the discount rate by mistake. Re-check: discount rate is the **cost of capital**, not the project's return.
> 3. **Payback Period confusion** — For unequal cash flows, students must compute **cumulative cash flows year-by-year**, not divide by average. Marks are awarded for the **table of cumulative CFs**.
> 4. **Sign errors in BCR** — Benefits must be in the numerator (positive); costs in the denominator. Inverting this reverses the decision rule.
> 5. **Ignoring salvage value** — Many students drop salvage in NPV computation. **Always include it as a year-n positive cash flow.**
> 6. **Currency unit missing** — Write *₹ Lakhs* or *₹* explicitly. "8.68" alone is incomplete.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (must memorize):**
>
> - **NPV** = $\sum CF_t / (1+r)^t - I_0$; accept if NPV ≥ 0.
> - **IRR** = rate that makes NPV = 0; accept if IRR ≥ cost of capital.
> - **BCR** = PV of Benefits / PV of Costs; accept if BCR ≥ 1.
> - **PI** = PV of future CFs / $I_0$; accept if PI ≥ 1.
> - **Payback** = $I_0$ / Annual CF (simple); use cumulative DCF table (discounted).
> - **Break-Even Quantity** = $FC / (p - v)$; always use contribution margin.
> - **Margin of Safety** = Actual Sales − BEP Sales; expressed in units or %.
> - **Sensitivity Ratio** = (% change in NPV) / (% change in input); > 1 means **highly sensitive**.
> - **Standard agro-project discount rates:** **8%–12%** for NABARD-subsidized projects, **12%–18%** for private commercial finance.
> - **Always state decision rule** at end of every computation.
> - **Always specify units** (₹ Lakhs, tonnes, years, %).
> - **Agro-context rule:** Always tie analysis back to the agri-product (dairy, milling, cold chain, plantation) — generic answers lose marks.
> - **Salvage value** must be discounted to year 0 and added to NPV.
> - **Inflation** is handled implicitly via the **nominal discount rate**; do not double-discount.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Acquiring and Allocating Funds
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT865 - AGRO-INDUSTRIAL PROJECT PLANNING AND MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Acquiring and Allocating Funds # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Acquiring and Allocating Funds** in the context of agro-industrial project planning refers to the strategic financial management process encompassing two interdependent pillars: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Acquisition of Funds:** The systematic identification, mobilization, and procurement of financial resources (capital) from various internal and external sources required to establish, operate, expand, or sustain an agro-industrial enterprise. > > **Allocation of Funds:** The disciplined deployment of acquired capital across competing project components—fixed assets, working capital, R&D, marketing, and contingencies—to maximize returns while minimizing financial risk. In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, this topic bridges **Project Finance (Module 2)** with the broader **Project Life Cycle** (Module 1), forming the financial backbone of agro-industrial ventures ranging from food processing plants and dairy units to biofertilizer manufacturing facilities. The discipline draws from three foundational areas: 1. **Corporate Finance Theory** – Cost of Capital, Capital Structure, Working Capital Management 2. **Development Banking Practice** – Term lending norms of NABARD, SIDBI, IFCI, and commercial banks 3. **Project Appraisal Frameworks** – UNIDO, World Bank, and ADB methodologies adapted for agricultural value chains ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **🍅 The Tomato Ketchup Factory Analogy** Imagine you want to start a **tomato ketchup factory** in Thrissur that processes 50 tonnes of tomatoes daily. **Acquiring Funds = Filling the Water Tank Before Irrigation** Think of money as **water** in a large overhead tank. Before the tomato plants (your project) can grow, you must fill this tank. Where does the water come from? - Your own savings (Equity) - A loan from the Kerala Gramin Bank (Debt) - A subsidy from MIDH (Government Grant) - Money from your cousin who believes in your idea (Venture Capital) Each source has a **"cost"** — just like different water sources have different mineral contents and pumping costs. Borewell water is "expensive" (high cost debt), but rainwater harvesting is "free" (retained earnings). Mixing them wisely gives the **best blend**. **Allocating Funds = Deciding Which Crop Gets How Much Water** Once the tank is full, you cannot water every plant equally. You must decide: - How much for the **land** (Land & Building) — say 30% - How much for the **drip system** (Machinery) — say 45% - How much for **seeds & fertilizer this month** (Working Capital) — say 15% - How much kept aside for a **drought** (Contingency) — say 10% If you over-irrigate the land and starve the seeds, the crop fails. The same logic applies to **capital budgeting** — misallocation leads to project failure even when funds are "abundant." ## 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot | Term | Meaning in Agro-Industrial Context | |---|---| | **Equity Capital** | Owner's risk capital — never returned, earns dividends | | **Debt Capital** | Borrowed funds — repaid with interest (e.g., NABARD refinance) | | **Subsidy** | Non-repayable government assistance (e.g., 35% back-ended subsidy under PMKSY) | | **Working Capital** | Short-term funds for daily operations (buying raw tomatoes, paying wages) | | **Opportunity Cost of Capital (OCC)** | The return foregone by not investing in the next best alternative (typically **12%–15%** for agro-industries in India) | | **Debt-Equity Ratio** | Proportion of borrowed to owned funds — KTU benchmark: **1.5:1 to 2:1** for agro-processing | | **Promoter's Contribution** | Mandatory owner's share — usually **25%–40%** of project cost | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Metric Highlight:** For most KTU numerical problems, the **Minimum Attractive Rate of Return (MARR)** or **Cut-off Rate** for agro-industrial projects is assumed to be **15%** unless otherwise specified, reflecting the opportunity cost in the Indian agricultural sector. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Fund Acquisition Agro-industrial projects draw capital from a structured hierarchy of sources. Each has distinct cost, risk, and tenure characteristics. ### Pillar 1: Equity Capital (Owned Funds) **Definition:** Permanent capital raised by issuing shares or contributed by promoters. It bears no mandatory dividend and no fixed repayment obligation. **Sub-categories:** - **Promoter's Equity** — must be ≥ **25%** of project cost for term loans up to ₹10 crore (NABARD norm) - **Public Issue / IPO** — for large agro-corporates (e.g., KRBL, Heritage Foods) - **Private Equity / Venture Capital** — for agtech startups (e.g., Ninjacart funding) - **Foreign Direct Investment (FDI)** — 100% allowed in food processing under automatic route > [!NOTE] > **Why Equity is the Most Expensive Source Despite "No Interest":** > Although equity has no contractual interest payment, shareholders demand a higher return (typically **18%–22% p.a.**) because they bear the highest risk — last to be paid in liquidation. This expected return is the **Cost of Equity (Ke)**. ### Pillar 2: Debt Capital (Borrowed Funds) **Definition:** Long-term loans repayable with interest. Classified by source: | Source | Typical Tenure | Interest Range | Best For | |---|---|---|---| | **NABARD Refinance** | 5–15 yrs | 8.5%–10.5% | Rural agro-units, dairy, food processing | | **SIDBI** | 3–7 yrs | 10%–12% | MSMEs, small food parks | | **Commercial Banks** | 5–10 yrs | 9%–13% | Working capital, term loans | | **IFCI / IDBI** | 7–15 yrs | 10%–11.5% | Large infrastructure projects | | **Bonds / Debentures** | 5–10 yrs | 9%–11% | Listed agro-corporates | | **Foreign Currency Loans** | 5–15 yrs | LIBOR + 3–5% | Large export-oriented units | ### Pillar 3: Government Subsidies & Grants These are **non-dilutive capital** — money received without ownership transfer or repayment. - **PMKSY (Pradhan Mantri Kisan Sampada Yojana)** — 35% grant for mega food parks - **MIDH (Mission for Integrated Development of Horticulture)** — 50% subsidy for cold storage - **PMFME (PM Formalization of Micro Food Processing Enterprises)** — 35% credit-linked subsidy - **NABARD RIDF (Rural Infrastructure Development Fund)** — for rural infrastructure - **State-level incentives** — Kerala State Industrial Development Corporation (KSIDC) support > [!IMPORTANT] > **Subsidy Trap (KTU Examiner's Favourite Question!):** > While subsidies reduce effective project cost, they often come with conditions: location restrictions, employment quotas, technology lock-ins, and back-ended disbursement (released only after 2–3 years). Always treat subsidy as **"uncertain inflow"** in cash flow projections. ### Pillar 4: Internal Accruals & Retained Earnings Profits ploughed back into the business. Cost is the **opportunity cost** to shareholders of not receiving dividends. ### Pillar 5: Hybrid Instruments - **Convertible Debentures** — debt that converts to equity after a milestone - **Preference Shares** — fixed dividend, repaid before equity in liquidation - **Mezzanine Finance** — subordinated debt with equity kickers ## 2.2 The Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC) This is the **single most important formula** in fund acquisition strategy. It represents the blended cost of all capital sources and serves as the **hurdle rate** for project acceptance. $$ WACC = \left( \frac{E}{V} \right) \cdot K_e + \left( \frac{D}{V} \right) \cdot K_d \cdot (1 - t) $$ Where: - $E$ = Market value of equity, $D$ = Market value of debt - $V = E + D$ = Total capitalization - $K_e$ = Cost of equity (%) - $K_d$ = Cost of debt before tax (%) - $t$ = Effective corporate tax rate (%) **Numerical Convention for Agro-Industries (KTU Assumption):** - $K_e = 18\%$, $K_d = 12\%$, $t = 30\%$ (corporate tax slab) - Typical debt-equity mix = 1.5:1 → E/V = 0.4, D/V = 0.6 ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Typical Range / Unit | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)** | $WACC = (E/V)K_e + (D/V)K_d(1-t)$ | Expressed as % p.a. | Discount rate for NPV | | 2 | **Cost of Equity (CAPM)** | $K_e = R_f + \beta (R_m - R_f)$ | 15%–22% for agro-stocks | Required return on equity | | 3 | **After-tax Cost of Debt** | $K_d^{AT} = K_d \times (1 - t)$ | 7%–9% post-tax | Effective borrowing cost | | 4 | **Debt-Equity Ratio** | $D/E$ | 1:1 to 2:1 | Financial leverage check | | 5 | **Interest Coverage Ratio** | $EBIT / \text{Interest Expense}$ | $\geq 3$ is healthy | Debt-servicing capacity | | 6 | **Net Present Value (NPV)** | $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - I_0$ | Currency units (₹) | Project viability test | | 7 | **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** | $NPV = 0$ at $r = IRR$ | % p.a. | Yield benchmarking | | 8 | **Payback Period** | $PP = \frac{I_0}{\text{Annual Cash Inflow}}$ | Years | Liquidity screening | | 9 | **Working Capital (Operating Cycle)** | $WC = COGS \times \left(\frac{\text{Inventory Days} + \text{Receivable Days} - \text{Payable Days}}{365}\right)$ | Currency units | Liquidity buffer needed | | 10 | **Return on Investment (ROI)** | $ROI = \frac{\text{Net Profit}}{\text{Total Investment}} \times 100$ | % | Fund-use efficiency | | 11 | **Degree of Financial Leverage (DFL)** | $DFL = \frac{EBIT}{EBT} = \frac{EBIT}{EBIT - I}$ | Dimensionless | Risk magnification | | 12 | **Operating Leverage (DOL)** | $DOL = \frac{Q(S-VC)}{Q(S-VC)-FC}$ | Dimensionless | Fixed cost burden | | 13 | **Breakeven Quantity** | $Q_{BE} = \frac{FC}{P - VC}$ | Units (kg, tonnes) | Minimum production | | 14 | **Promoter's Contribution** | $\frac{\text{Promoter Equity}}{\text{Total Project Cost}}$ | $\geq 25\%$ (NABARD norm) | Loan eligibility | | 15 | **Subsidy-Adjusted Effective Cost** | $C_{eff} = \frac{I_0 - S}{I_0}$ where $S$ = subsidy | Ratio | Real capital burden | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility This theoretical framework drives **every agro-industrial investment decision** in India: - **Cold Storage Project (₹5 crore, Manipur):** A promoter combines ₹1.5 cr own funds + ₹3 cr NABARD loan + ₹1.5 cr MIDH subsidy. WACC calculation determines if the cold storage is viable vs. an alternate rice mill. - **Dairy Cooperative (Karnataka):** Decides between equity from milk producer-members vs. bank loan — affects voting rights and dividend policies. - **Rice Milling Cluster (Andhra Pradesh):** Uses PMKSY grant to reduce effective capital cost by 35%, making IRR jump from 14% (unviable) to 19% (highly viable). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** In agro-industrial projects, **Working Capital is disproportionately large** (often 25%–30% of project cost) because of seasonality — tomatoes are harvested for 90 days but ketchup is sold for 365 days. This makes **fund allocation** more critical than in continuous-process industries like cement or steel. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Weighted Average Cost of Capital ### Problem Setup A Kerala-based **spice processing unit** (cardamom, pepper, turmeric) requires a total project outlay of **₹100 lakh**. The financing plan is: - **Equity (E):** ₹40 lakh, with expected dividend yield = 20% p.a. - **Term Loan (D):** ₹60 lakh, interest rate = 14% p.a. - **Corporate Tax Rate (t):** 30% - **Promoter expects IRR ≥ 16%** **Objective:** Determine WACC and verify if the project meets the hurdle rate. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Identify capital weights** $$ V = E + D = 40 + 60 = 100 \text{ lakh} $$ $$ \frac{E}{V} = \frac{40}{100} = 0.40 $$ $$ \frac{D}{V} = \frac{60}{100} = 0.60 $$ **Step 2 — Identify component costs** Cost of equity (from expected dividend yield): $$ K_e = 20\% = 0.20 $$ Cost of debt (before tax): $$ K_d = 14\% = 0.14 $$ Corporate tax rate: $$ t = 30\% = 0.30 $$ **Step 3 — Compute after-tax cost of debt** $$ K_d^{AT} = K_d \times (1 - t) = 0.14 \times (1 - 0.30) $$ $$ K_d^{AT} = 0.14 \times 0.70 = 0.0980 = 9.80\% $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why we multiply by (1 − t):** Interest paid to lenders is an **expense deductible** from taxable income. So for every ₹100 of interest, the company effectively pays only ₹70 (because ₹30 of tax is saved). This tax shield is a major reason why debt is preferred over equity, up to a leverage threshold. **Step 4 — Compute WACC** $$ WACC = \left(\frac{E}{V}\right) K_e + \left(\frac{D}{V}\right) K_d^{AT} $$ $$ WACC = (0.40 \times 0.20) + (0.60 \times 0.0980) $$ $$ WACC = 0.0800 + 0.0588 = 0.1388 $$ $$ \boxed{WACC = 13.88\%} $$ **Step 5 — Decision rule** Since computed WACC = **13.88%** is **less than** the required return of **16%**, the project will generate positive NPV when discounted at WACC, and is therefore **financially viable**. ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Working Capital Requirement (Operating Cycle Method) ### Problem Setup An agro-industrial unit processing **cashew nuts** in Kollam has the following annual operating data: - **Cost of Goods Sold (COGS):** ₹500 lakh/year - **Raw material inventory holding period:** 60 days - **Work-in-progress period:** 30 days - **Finished goods holding period:** 20 days - **Credit allowed to customers (receivables):** 45 days - **Credit availed from suppliers (payables):** 30 days - **Cash buffer for wages & overheads:** 15 days **Objective:** Compute total working capital requirement. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Calculate Operating Cycle (OC)** The Operating Cycle consists of **inventory days + receivable days**, then we subtract **payable days** to get the **cash conversion cycle (CCC)**: $$ OC = \text{RM Days} + \text{WIP Days} + \text{FG Days} + \text{Receivable Days} $$ $$ OC = 60 + 30 + 20 + 45 = 155 \text{ days} $$ **Step 2 — Net Operating Cycle (Cash Conversion Cycle)** $$ CCC = OC - \text{Payable Days} = 155 - 30 = 125 \text{ days} $$ **Step 3 — Add Cash Buffer** $$ \text{Total WC Days} = CCC + \text{Cash Buffer} = 125 + 15 = 140 \text{ days} $$ **Step 4 — Compute Working Capital in ₹** Daily COGS: $$ \text{Daily COGS} = \frac{500}{365} = 1.3699 \text{ lakh/day} $$ Total Working Capital: $$ WC = 1.3699 \times 140 = 191.78 \text{ lakh} \approx \text{₹191.78 lakh} $$ $$ \boxed{WC \approx \text{₹191.78 lakh (or ₹1.92 crore)}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation:** Out of the total project cost (say ₹10 crore), nearly **20% must be earmarked as working capital** — a typical figure for agro-processing. This is why banks provide **separate cash credit (CC) limits** for working capital, distinct from term loans for fixed assets. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Fund Allocation Decision Framework The following matrix helps decide **how to allocate** acquired funds across project components. | Allocation Bucket | % of Project Cost (Typical) | Source of Funds | Liquidity | Risk Class | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Land & Site Development** | 10%–15% | Promoter equity (long-tenure) | Illiquid | Low risk | | **Building & Civil Works** | 20%–30% | Term loan + equity | Illiquid | Low risk | | **Plant & Machinery** | 35%–45% | Term loan (NABARD refinance) | Illiquid | Medium risk | | **Pre-operative Expenses** | 3%–5% | Equity | Sunk cost | High risk | | **Margin Money for WC** | 5%–8% | Equity (mandatory) | Liquid buffer | Medium risk | | **Working Capital (CC limit)** | 20%–30% of annual sales | Cash credit facility | Highly liquid | High risk | | **Contingency Reserve** | 5%–10% | Retained earnings / equity | Liquid reserve | Insurance | | **R&D / Quality Lab** | 2%–4% | Equity + grants | Intangible | High risk, high reward | | **Marketing & Brand Building** | 3%–6% | Equity | Intangible | High risk | ## 3.4 Exhaustive Derivation: Cost of Equity using CAPM The Capital Asset Pricing Model gives a more rigorous estimate of cost of equity than simple dividend yield. $$ K_e = R_f + \beta \times (R_m - R_f) $$ **Given for an agro-industrial stock:** - Risk-free rate $R_f = 7\%$ (10-year G-Sec yield) - Market return $R_m = 14\%$ (Nifty 50 long-term average) - Beta $\beta = 1.25$ (agricultural commodities are slightly more volatile than market) **Computation:** $$ K_e = 7\% + 1.25 \times (14\% - 7\%) $$ $$ K_e = 7\% + 1.25 \times 7\% $$ $$ K_e = 7\% + 8.75\% = 15.75\% $$ $$ \boxed{K_e = 15.75\%} $$ **Interpretation:** Even with a moderate beta, agro-industrial firms must offer **15.75% expected return** to attract equity investors, reflecting seasonal risks, weather dependence, and policy uncertainty. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Fund Acquisition & Allocation Flow in Agro-Industrial Project ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Project Initiation
Total Outlay: Rs X crore] --> nodeB{Fund Acquisition Strategy} nodeB --> nodeC[Equity Capital
Promoter 25% to 40%] nodeB --> nodeD[Debt Capital
Term Loans 40% to 60%] nodeB --> nodeE[Government Subsidies
PMKSY MIDH PMFME] nodeB --> nodeF[Internal Accruals
Retained Earnings] nodeB --> nodeG[Hybrid Instruments
Mezzanine Pref Shares] nodeC --> nodeH{WACC Computation} nodeD --> nodeH nodeE --> nodeH nodeF --> nodeH nodeG --> nodeH nodeH --> nodeI{Compare WACC vs Hurdle Rate
MARR equals 15%} nodeI -->|WACC below 15%| nodeJ[Project Viable
Proceed to Allocation] nodeI -->|WACC above 15%| nodeK[Restructure Capital Mix
Seek Subsidies] nodeJ --> nodeL{Fund Allocation Decision} nodeL --> nodeM[Fixed Asset Block
Land Building Machinery
60% to 80%] nodeL --> nodeN[Working Capital Block
Inventory Receivables
20% to 30%] nodeL --> nodeO[Contingency Reserve
5% to 10%] nodeL --> nodeP[Intangibles
R and D Brand
3% to 5%] nodeM --> nodeQ[Operations Begin
Revenue Generation] nodeN --> nodeQ nodeO --> nodeQ nodeP --> nodeQ nodeQ --> nodeR[Cash Flow Streams] nodeR --> nodeS[Debt Servicing Interest Principal] nodeR --> nodeT[Equity Dividend Capital Appreciation] nodeR --> nodeU[Reinvestment Capacity] nodeS --> nodeV[Monitor Leverage Ratios
Interest Coverage DSCR] nodeT --> nodeW[Monitor ROI EPS] nodeU --> nodeX[Expansion Phase
New Acquisition Cycle] nodeX --> nodeB ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Capital Structure Decision Tree (KTU Examination Focus) ```mermaid flowchart TD start[Capital Structure Decision] --> q1{Debt Equity Target Ratio} q1 -->|Conservative 1 to 1| pathA[Low Financial Risk
Lower EPS Volatility
Suitable for Seasonal Agro Units] q1 -->|Moderate 1.5 to 1| pathB[Balanced Approach
Recommended for Most KTU Cases
NABARD Norm] q1 -->|Aggressive 2 to 1| pathC[Higher Financial Leverage
Higher EPS in Good Years
Higher Bankruptcy Risk] pathA --> checkA{Interest Coverage
above 4} checkA -->|Yes| goA[Proceed with Term Loan] checkA -->|No| reviseA[Reduce Debt Component] pathB --> checkB{Interest Coverage
between 3 and 4} checkB -->|Yes| goB[Standard Bank Funding] checkB -->|No| reviseB[Convert to Mezzanine] pathC --> checkC{Interest Coverage
above 5 needed} checkC -->|Yes| goC[Aggressive Expansion Mode] checkC -->|No| reviseC[Refinance with Equity] goA --> final[Final Capital Structure Locked] goB --> final goC --> final reviseA --> start reviseB --> start reviseC --> start ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Subsidy Disbursement Mechanism ```mermaid flowchart LR sponsor[Project Sponsor
Agro Processor] --> app[Submit DPR
Detailed Project Report] app --> bank[Bank Appraisal
Technical Financial] bank --> rec[Sanction Letter
with Subsidy Tag] rec --> nodal[Nodal Agency
NABARD or SFAC] nodal --> verify[Site Verification
and Inspection] verify --> tpa[Third Party Agency
Inspection Report] tpa --> approve[Subsidy Approval
By Ministry] approve --> disburse[Funds Disbursed
To Bank Account] disburse --> bank2[Bank Adjusts
Loan Principal] bank2 --> sponsor2[Sponsor Receives
Net Reduced Liability] style sponsor fill:#e1f5ff style sponsor2 fill:#d4edda style approve fill:#fff3cd style disburse fill:#f8d7da ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Diagram Insight:** Notice how the sponsor **never directly receives subsidy cash**. It is routed through the lending bank as **adjustment against loan principal**. This is called **back-ended subsidy** — the sponsor benefits via reduced future interest outflow, not immediate liquidity. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (Short Answer) **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** > **Q: Define "Weighted Average Cost of Capital" (WACC). Why is it used as the discount rate in capital budgeting decisions for agro-industrial projects?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC) is the blended cost of all sources of long-term finance (equity, debt, preference capital) weighted by their proportion in the total capital structure. Mathematically, $$ WACC = \left(\frac{E}{V}\right)K_e + \left(\frac{D}{V}\right)K_d(1-t) $$ **Use as Discount Rate (1.5 marks):** WACC represents the **minimum expected return** that a project must earn to satisfy all capital providers. In agro-industrial capital budgeting, using WACC as the discount rate ensures that: - Net Present Value (NPV) calculation reflects the **true opportunity cost** of capital tied up in seasonal operations. - Projects with IRR greater than WACC create shareholder value. - It accounts for the **tax shield benefit** of debt — especially important for agro-units with high debt-equity ratios sanctioned by NABARD. --- ### Question 2 (Short Answer) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** > **Q: List any four sources of long-term finance available to agro-industrial enterprises in India.** **Model Answer (3 marks — 0.75 each):** 1. **Promoter's Equity Capital** — Risk capital contributed by owners; minimum 25% required for NABARD-backed term loans. 2. **Term Loans from NABARD Refinance** — Long-term loans (5–15 years) at concessional rates (8.5%–10.5%) for agro-processing, dairy, and cold chain projects. 3. **Government Subsidies** — Non-repayable grants under schemes like PMKSY (35%), MIDH (50%), PMFME (35%) reducing effective capital cost. 4. **Issue of Debentures/Bonds** — Listed agro-corporates (e.g., sugar mills, edible oil refiners) raise debt via secured redeemable debentures with 9%–11% coupon rates. *(Alternative valid sources: Preference shares, Foreign Direct Investment under automatic route, Mezzanine finance from SIDBI, Venture Capital for agri-startups.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (14 Marks) — Capital Structure & WACC Computation **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** > **A coconut oil extraction unit in Kerala requires a total investment of ₹200 lakh. The financing plan is as follows:** > > - **Equity Capital:** ₹80 lakh (expected dividend yield = 18%) > - **Term Loan from NABARD:** ₹100 lakh (interest = 11% p.a.) > - **Subsidy from MIDH:** ₹20 lakh (non-repayable, received in Year 2) > - **Corporate Tax Rate:** 30% > > **(a) Compute the Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC) considering only equity and debt.** [7 marks] > > **(b) If the project generates an annual cash inflow of ₹35 lakh for 10 years, evaluate its viability using NPV at WACC. Should the promoter proceed?** [7 marks] #### Model Solution **Part (a) — WACC Computation [7 marks]** Step 1 — Identify weights (ignoring subsidy as it is non-dilutive): $$ V = E + D = 80 + 100 = 180 \text{ lakh} $$ (Note: Subsidy of ₹20 lakh reduces effective project cost but does not form part of capital structure carrying a "cost.") $$ \frac{E}{V} = \frac{80}{180} = 0.4444 $$ $$ \frac{D}{V} = \frac{100}{180} = 0.5556 $$ Step 2 — Component costs: $$ K_e = 18\% = 0.18 $$ $$ K_d = 11\% = 0.11 $$ Step 3 — After-tax cost of debt: $$ K_d^{AT} = 0.11 \times (1 - 0.30) = 0.0770 = 7.70\% $$ Step 4 — WACC: $$ WACC = (0.4444 \times 0.18) + (0.5556 \times 0.0770) $$ $$ WACC = 0.0800 + 0.0428 = 0.1228 $$ $$ \boxed{WACC = 12.28\%} $$ --- **Part (b) — NPV Evaluation [7 marks]** Step 1 — Present Value Annuity Factor at 12.28% for 10 years: $$ PVAF(12.28\%, 10) = \frac{1 - (1.1228)^{-10}}{0.1228} $$ Step 2 — Compute discount factor per year: Year 1: $(1.1228)^{-1} = 0.8907$ Year 2: $(1.1228)^{-2} = 0.7933$ Year 3: $(1.1228)^{-3} = 0.7065$ Year 4: $(1.1228)^{-4} = 0.6293$ Year 5: $(1.1228)^{-5} = 0.5605$ Year 6: $(1.1228)^{-6} = 0.4992$ Year 7: $(1.1228)^{-7} = 0.4446$ Year 8: $(1.1228)^{-8} = 0.3960$ Year 9: $(1.1228)^{-9} = 0.3527$ Year 10: $(1.1228)^{-10} = 0.3141$ Sum: $$ PVAF = 5.5869 $$ Step 3 — Present Value of Cash Inflows: $$ PV = 35 \times 5.5869 = 195.54 \text{ lakh} $$ Step 4 — Net Present Value: $$ NPV = PV - I_0 = 195.54 - 200 = -4.46 \text{ lakh} $$ *(Note: Strictly, the ₹20 lakh subsidy received in Year 2 should be added back as a positive inflow at Year 2 discount factor: $20 \times 0.7933 = 15.87$ lakh. Adjusted NPV = $195.54 + 15.87 - 200 = +11.41$ lakh — viable when subsidy is included!)* **Conclusion:** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Without subsidy:** NPV = **−₹4.46 lakh** → **Reject the project** at pure WACC basis. > > **With subsidy inclusion:** NPV = **+₹11.41 lakh** → **Project is viable.** > > **Decision:** Promoter should proceed, **provided the subsidy is firm and disbursed as scheduled.** This demonstrates the **critical role of subsidies** in making marginal agro-industrial projects financially attractive. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Working Capital & Fund Allocation **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** > **A fruit pulp processing unit in Kodagu (Karnataka) has the following annual data:** > > - **Cost of Goods Sold (COGS):** ₹1,460 lakh > - **Raw material inventory days:** 90 days > - **Work-in-progress days:** 15 days > - **Finished goods storage days:** 20 days > - **Credit period allowed to buyers:** 60 days > - **Credit period availed from fruit suppliers:** 30 days > - **Cash buffer for wages/overheads:** 25 days > > **(a) Compute the Gross and Net Operating Cycle. Calculate the total working capital requirement in ₹ lakhs.** [7 marks] > > **(b) If the total project cost is ₹1,000 lakh, what percentage must be allocated to working capital margin? Is this allocation adequate for a cash credit limit of ₹400 lakh from the bank?** [7 marks] #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Operating Cycle & WC [7 marks]** Step 1 — Gross Operating Cycle (GOC): $$ GOC = \text{RM} + \text{WIP} + \text{FG} + \text{Receivables} $$ $$ GOC = 90 + 15 + 20 + 60 = 185 \text{ days} $$ Step 2 — Net Operating Cycle (NOC): $$ NOC = GOC - \text{Payables} = 185 - 30 = 155 \text{ days} $$ Step 3 — Total Working Capital Days: $$ WC_{days} = NOC + \text{Cash Buffer} = 155 + 25 = 180 \text{ days} $$ Step 4 — Daily COGS: $$ \text{Daily COGS} = \frac{1460}{365} = 4.00 \text{ lakh/day} $$ Step 5 — Working Capital Requirement: $$ WC = 4.00 \times 180 = 720 \text{ lakh} $$ $$ \boxed{WC = \text{₹720 lakh (₹7.20 crore)}} $$ --- **Part (b) — Allocation Adequacy [7 marks]** Step 1 — Percentage allocation: $$ \% WC = \frac{720}{1000} \times 100 = 72\% $$ Step 2 — Cash Credit (CC) Limit Analysis: Banks typically require a **margin of 25%** on the working capital gap, meaning: $$ \text{Promoter margin required} = 25\% \times 720 = 180 \text{ lakh} $$ Step 3 — CC Limit Calculation: $$ \text{CC Limit} = 75\% \times 720 = 540 \text{ lakh} $$ Step 4 — Comparison with available CC limit of ₹400 lakh: $$ \text{Shortfall} = 540 - 400 = 140 \text{ lakh} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students compute WC as 72% of project cost and conclude "WC allocation is more than adequate." This is WRONG. The correct interpretation is that the **bank's CC limit of ₹400 lakh is INADEQUATE** — there is a **₹140 lakh shortfall**. The promoter must either: > - Arrange additional CC from a second bank (multiple banking arrangement), > - Inject more promoter contribution as WC margin, > - Reduce inventory days through better cold chain management. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > 1. **Always show WACC in percentage form AND decimal form** — students frequently mix units and lose 1 mark. > 2. **Subsidies must be clearly identified** as non-cost capital; do NOT include them in WACC numerator. > 3. **Tax rate of 30% is mandatory** in agro-industrial calculations unless problem specifies otherwise. > 4. **Discount factor summation** is required in NPV — shortcut formulas without derivation lose 2 marks. > 5. **Mention NABARD/PMKSY** wherever applicable — examiners reward domain-specific terminology. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### ⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist **Core Concepts (must know verbatim):** - [ ] WACC formula and its three components - [ ] Debt-Equity Ratio: 1.5:1 is the KTU benchmark for agro-industries - [ ] Promoter's Contribution: minimum **25%** of project cost (NABARD norm) - [ ] Minimum Attractive Rate of Return (MARR): **15%** for agro-industrial projects **Formulae to Memorize:** - [ ] WACC = $(E/V) K_e + (D/V) K_d (1-t)$ - [ ] $K_d^{AT} = K_d \times (1-t)$ - [ ] $NPV = \sum CF_t / (1+r)^t - I_0$ - [ ] Working Capital = COGS × WC Days / 365 **Subsidies to Remember (frequent in questions):** - [ ] PMKSY — 35% grant for mega food parks - [ ] MIDH — 50% for cold storage - [ ] PMFME — 35% for micro food enterprises - [ ] NABARD RIDF — rural infrastructure **Common Mistakes to Avoid:** - [ ] ❌ Treating subsidy as part of cost-bearing capital - [ ] ❌ Forgetting tax shield on debt interest - [ ] ❌ Confusing operating cycle with cash conversion cycle - [ ] ❌ Using book value instead of market value for WACC weights - [ ] ❌ Ignoring seasonality factor in agro WC estimates **Decision Rules (gold for exams):** - [ ] ✅ Accept project if NPV > 0 at WACC - [ ] ✅ Accept project if IRR > WACC - [ ] ✅ Accept project if PI (Profitability Index) > 1 - [ ] ✅ Maintain Interest Coverage Ratio ≥ 3 - [ ] ✅ Maintain Debt Service Coverage Ratio (DSCR) ≥ 1.5 **Key Indian Institutions (mention in answers for full marks):** - [ ] NABARD — apex agri-finance institution - [ ] SIDBI — small industries funding - [ ] IFCI — industrial finance corporation - [ ] SFAC — Small Farmers Agribusiness Consortium - [ ] APEDA — Agricultural & Processed Food Products Export Development Authority

topics.title: Cash Flow Statement - Balance Sheet
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT865 - AGRO-INDUSTRIAL PROJECT PLANNING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cash Flow Statement - Balance Sheet

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Balance Sheet — Definition

A **Balance Sheet** is a formal, point-in-time financial statement that presents the complete financial position of an agro-industrial enterprise by reporting its **Assets**, **Liabilities**, and **Owner's Equity** at a specific date (typically the last day of the financial year). It obeys the universally accepted double-entry bookkeeping identity and is, therefore, always "balanced."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition:**
> *"A Balance Sheet is a financial statement that summarizes a firm's assets, liabilities, and shareholders' equity at a given point in time, used to evaluate the financial health and capital structure of an agro-industrial project."*

The underlying **Accounting Equation** is:

$$\text{Assets} = \text{Liabilities} + \text{Owner's Equity}$$

### 1.2 The Cash Flow Statement — Definition

A **Cash Flow Statement** is a period-based financial report that tracks the actual **movement of cash and cash equivalents** entering and leaving an agro-industrial project over a defined accounting period. It categorizes these cash movements into three standard activities: **Operating**, **Investing**, and **Financing**.

> [!NOTE]
