# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**Q: Define the term "Break Even Point" with reference to an agro-industrial project. State its significance in project feasibility evaluation.**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The **Break Even Point (BEP)** is the level of production and sales at which the **Total Revenue** of an agro-industrial project exactly equals its **Total Cost**, resulting in **zero profit and zero loss**. Mathematically, BEP is the condition $\text{TR} = \text{TC}$, which yields the break even quantity as:

$$
Q_{BE} = \frac{\text{FC}}{p - v}
$$

**Significance in Project Feasibility:**
1. It indicates the **minimum production volume** required for the agro-unit to survive.
2. It helps financiers (e.g., **NABARD**, commercial banks) assess the **debt-servicing capacity** of the project.
3. It enables comparison between **alternative technologies** (e.g., traditional vs. modern rice milling) by comparing their break even quantities.
4. It quantifies the **risk exposure** through the Margin of Safety and helps in pricing and capacity decisions.

---

### Question A2
**Q: Differentiate between Fixed Cost and Variable Cost. Give two examples of each from an agro-processing industry.**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Fixed Cost | Variable Cost |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Costs that remain unchanged irrespective of the level of output (within the relevant range). | Costs that vary in direct proportion to the volume of production. |
| Behaviour with output | Constant; does not change with $Q$. | Increases or decreases linearly with $Q$. |
| Per-unit behaviour | Decreases per unit as $Q$ rises (due to spreading). | Remains constant per unit. |
| Examples in agro-industry | (i) Depreciation of a sugarcane crusher. (ii) Salary of a permanent quality control supervisor. | (i) Cost of sugarcane procured per quintal. (ii) Packaging material cost per litre of milk. |

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**

**(a)** The following data relates to a proposed **banana chips manufacturing unit**:

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Annual Fixed Cost | ₹ 4,50,000 |
| Selling Price per kg | ₹ 90 |
| Variable Cost per kg | ₹ 60 |
| Expected Annual Sales | 12,000 kg |

**Compute:**
(i) The Break Even Point in units (kg) and in sales value (₹).
(ii) The expected annual profit.
(iii) The Margin of Safety in units and as a percentage.

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neatly labelled **Break Even Chart**, explain the concept of Break Even Point. Discuss the practical utility of Break Even Analysis in planning an agro-industrial enterprise.

**(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question A(a):

**Step 1: Identify Given Parameters**
- Fixed Cost (FC) = ₹ 4,50,000
- Selling Price ($p$) = ₹ 90/kg
- Variable Cost ($v$) = ₹ 60/kg
- Contribution Margin (CM) = $p - v = 90 - 60$ = ₹ 30/kg
- Expected Sales ($Q_{\text{actual}}$) = 12,000 kg

**Step 2: Compute BEP in Units**

Apply the master formula:

$$
Q_{BE} = \frac{\text{FC}}{p - v} = \frac{4{,}50{,}000}{30}
$$

$$
Q_{BE} = 15{,}000 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 3: Compute BEP in Sales Value**

$$
\text{BEP}_{₹} = p \times Q_{BE} = 90 \times 15{,}000
$$

$$
\text{BEP}_{₹} = \text{₹ } 13{,}50{,}000
$$

**Step 4: Compute Expected Profit**

Use the profit function:

$$
\pi = (p - v) \times Q_{\text{actual}} - \text{FC}
$$

$$
\pi = 30 \times 12{,}000 - 4{,}50{,}000
$$

$$
\pi = 3{,}60{,}000 - 4{,}50{,}000 = -90{,}000
$$

The negative value indicates a **loss of ₹ 90,000** because expected sales (12,000 kg) are below the BEP (15,000 kg).

**Step 5: Compute Margin of Safety**

In units:

$$
\text{MOS}_{\text{units}} = Q_{\text{actual}} - Q_{BE} = 12{,}000 - 15{,}000 = -3{,}000 \text{ kg}
$$

In sales value:

$$
\text{Actual Sales Value} = 12{,}000 \times 90 = \text{₹ } 10{,}80{,}000
$$

$$
\text{MOS}_{₹} = 10{,}80{,}000 - 13{,}50{,}000 = -\text{₹ } 2{,}70{,}000
$$

As a percentage (using absolute actual sales):

$$
\text{MOS Ratio} = \frac{-2{,}70{,}000}{10{,}80{,}000} \times 100 = -25\%
$$

**Interpretation:** The unit is operating **25% below** its break even level. Management must either increase sales volume to at least 15,000 kg, or reduce fixed costs, or raise the selling price.

---

#### Solution to Question A(b):

**Break Even Chart Explanation:**

A Break Even Chart is a graphical representation of the **Cost-Volume-Profit (CVP)** relationships of a firm. The chart has the following components:

1. **X-axis:** Represents the volume of output (units of banana chips in kg).
2. **Y-axis:** Represents the cost and revenue in rupees.
3. **Total Revenue Line (TR):** A straight line starting from the origin with slope equal to the selling price (₹ 90/kg).
4. **Total Cost Line (TC):** A straight line starting from a positive Y-intercept equal to Fixed Cost (₹ 4,50,000) with slope equal to the variable cost per unit (₹ 60/kg).
5. **Fixed Cost Line:** A horizontal line at the level of FC.
6. **Intersection Point:** The point where TR and TC intersect is the **Break Even Point**, with coordinates $(15{,}000 \text{ kg}, \text{₹ } 13{,}50{,}000)$.
7. **Loss Area:** The triangular region to the left of BEP, bounded by TR and TC.
8. **Profit Area:** The triangular region to the right of BEP, bounded by TR and TC.

**Practical Utility in Agro-Industrial Enterprise Planning:**

| Utility | Description |
|---|---|
| **Profit Planning** | BEP tells the entrepreneur how many kg of banana chips must be sold to start earning profit. |
| **Pricing Decisions** | Helps determine the minimum viable selling price. |
| **Capacity Decisions** | Compares BEP with installed capacity to assess under-utilisation. |
| **Loan Appraisal** | Banks use BEP to decide whether to extend credit to agro-units. |
| **Cost Control** | Shows how changes in FC or VC affect the break even level. |
| **Seasonal Risk Assessment** | Quantifies how a short operating season (e.g., mango pulp processing only in summer) affects profitability. |

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**

**(a)** A small-scale **oil extraction unit** produces two products — **mustard oil (Product M)** and **refined soybean oil (Product S)**. The annual fixed cost is ₹ 8,00,000. The data are:

| Parameter | Product M | Product S |
|---|---|---|
| Selling Price per litre | ₹ 180 | ₹ 150 |
| Variable Cost per litre | ₹ 120 | ₹ 110 |
| Sales Mix Proportion | 0.6 | 0.4 |

**Compute:**
(i) The Break Even Point for each product in litres.
(ii) The overall Break Even Point in total sales value (₹).

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What are the **assumptions and limitations** of Break Even Analysis? How can these limitations be addressed when applying BEA to agro-industrial projects?

**(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question B(a):

**Step 1: Compute Contribution Margin for Each Product**

For Product M (Mustard Oil):

$$
\text{CM}_M = p_M - v_M = 180 - 120 = \text{₹ } 60/\text{litre}
$$

For Product S (Soybean Oil):

$$
\text{CM}_S = p_S - v_S = 150 - 110 = \text{₹ } 40/\text{litre}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Weighted Average Contribution Margin**

Using the sales mix proportions $w_M = 0.6$ and $w_S = 0.4$:

$$
\overline{\text{CM}} = (w_M \times \text{CM}_M) + (w_S \times \text{CM}_S)
$$

$$
\overline{\text{CM}} = (0.6 \times 60) + (0.4 \times 40)
$$

$$
\overline{\text{CM}} = 36 + 16 = \text{₹ } 52/\text{litre (composite)}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Composite BEP Quantity**

$$
Q_{BE}^{\text{composite}} = \frac{\text{FC}}{\overline{\text{CM}}} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{52}
$$

$$
Q_{BE}^{\text{composite}} \approx 15{,}384.62 \text{ litres (rounded)}
$$

Let us take $Q_{BE}^{\text{composite}} \approx 15{,}385$ litres.

**Step 4: Allocate BEP Quantity to Each Product According to Sales Mix**

For Product M (Mustard Oil):

$$
Q_{BE}^{M} = Q_{BE}^{\text{composite}} \times w_M = 15{,}385 \times 0.6
$$

$$
Q_{BE}^{M} \approx 9{,}231 \text{ litres}
$$

For Product S (Soybean Oil):

$$
Q_{BE}^{S} = Q_{BE}^{\text{composite}} \times w_S = 15{,}385 \times 0.4
$$

$$
Q_{BE}^{S} \approx 6{,}154 \text{ litres}
$$

**Step 5: Compute BEP in Sales Value**

Total BEP sales value:

$$
\text{BEP}_{₹} = (Q_{BE}^{M} \times p_M) + (Q_{BE}^{S} \times p_S)
$$

$$
\text{BEP}_{₹} = (9{,}231 \times 180) + (6{,}154 \times 150)
$$

$$
\text{BEP}_{₹} = 16{,}61{,}580 + 9{,}23{,}100 = \text{₹ } 25{,}84{,}680
$$

**Verification (Alternative Method):**

$$
\text{BEP}_{₹} = \frac{\text{FC}}{\text{Overall CM Ratio}}
$$

CM Ratio for M = $60/180 = 0.3333$
CM Ratio for S = $40/150 = 0.2667$

Overall CM Ratio:

$$
\text{Overall CM Ratio} = (0.6 \times 0.3333) + (0.4 \times 0.2667) = 0.2000 + 0.1067 = 0.3067
$$

Therefore:

$$
\text{BEP}_{₹} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{0.3067} \approx \text{₹ } 26{,}08{,}670
$$

(The minor difference is due to rounding of composite BEP units; the second method is the more accurate representation of BEP in sales value.)

---

#### Solution to Question B(b):

**Assumptions of Break Even Analysis:**

1. **Linear Cost and Revenue Functions:** Costs and revenues are assumed to vary linearly with output. In practice, agro-industrial units often face economies of scale (non-linear cost behaviour).
2. **Constant Selling Price:** The selling price per unit is assumed constant. However, agricultural commodity prices fluctuate seasonally and globally.
3. **Constant Sales Mix (Multi-product):** In multi-product BEA, the sales mix is assumed to remain constant.
4. **Fixed Costs Truly Fixed:** All fixed costs are assumed to be invariant with output. However, at very high output levels, additional capacity may be needed (step-fixed costs).
5. **Variable Cost per Unit Constant:** Variable cost per unit is assumed constant. In reality, bulk procurement discounts or supply shortages may alter unit variable cost.
6. **Single Product or Homogeneous Output:** Simplified BEA treats output as a single homogeneous product.
7. **Inventory Levels Unchanged:** BEA assumes that production equals sales (no inventory accumulation).

**Limitations of BEA:**

| Limitation | Impact on Agro-Industrial Application |
|---|---|
| Static Model | Does not capture dynamic seasonal effects (e.g., sugarcane crushing season of only 6 months). |
| Ignores Working Capital Cycle | Does not consider the timing of cash inflows and outflows, which is critical in agro-business. |
| Ignores Inflation | The cost and price parameters may shift due to inflation, distorting the BEP. |
| Difficulty in Cost Classification | Many agro-industry costs are semi-variable (e.g., electricity), making classification tricky. |
| Sales Price Volatility | Agricultural produce prices fluctuate due to MSP, market arrivals, and global trends. |
| External Risks | Climate change, pest attacks, and policy shifts can disrupt the cost-revenue assumptions. |

**How to Address the Limitations:**

1. **Sensitivity Analysis:** Recompute BEP under multiple scenarios (best case, base case, worst case) to understand the range of break even levels.
2. **Use of Computer Simulation:** Monte Carlo simulation can model probabilistic distributions of price and cost.
3. **Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Extension:** Combine BEA with Net Present Value (NPV) and Internal Rate of Return (IRR) to account for the time value of money.
4. **Periodic Review:** Update cost and price assumptions quarterly to reflect seasonal and market realities.
5. **Semi-Variable Cost Decomposition:** Use the high-low method or least-squares regression to split semi-variable costs into fixed and variable components.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Reasons for Mark Deduction in KTU Board Exams (BEA Module):**
> 
> 1. **Formula Misapplication (–2 marks):** Using the BEP formula $Q_{BE} = \text{FC} / \text{CM}$ but substituting **Total Variable Cost (TVC)** instead of **Variable Cost per unit ($v$)**. Always check the units of every variable before computing.
> 
> 2. **Multi-product Mix Error (–2 to –3 marks):** Failing to verify that the sales mix proportions sum to 1.0. If the mix is given in absolute sales units, first compute the proportion by dividing each product's sales by total sales.
> 
> 3. **Confusing BEP with MOS (–1 to –2 marks):** Margin of Safety is *additional sales above BEP*, not BEP itself.
> 
> 4. **Graph Labelling Inadequacy (–1 to –2 marks):** Drawing the BEA chart without labelling all four key lines (TR, TC, FC, and the axes) and without clearly marking the BEP intersection.
> 
> 5. **Ignoring "Profit = 0" Definition (–1 mark):** Not stating the BEP condition (TR = TC) explicitly before deriving the formula.
> 
> 6. **Unit Mismatch (–1 mark):** Writing BEP in kg but the question requested litres, or vice versa.
> 
> 7. **Assumptions not stated (–1 to –2 marks in theory parts):** Forgetting to mention the linearity assumption of the CVP model.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Break Even Analysis**

- [ ] **BEP Definition:** The volume of output where Total Revenue = Total Cost, yielding zero profit.
- [ ] **Master Formula (Units):** $Q_{BE} = \text{FC} / (p - v)$
- [ ] **Master Formula (₹):** $\text{BEP}_{₹} = \text{FC} / \text{CM Ratio}$
- [ ] **Contribution Margin:** $\text{CM} = p - v$; must be positive for survival.
- [ ] **CM Ratio:** $\text{CM Ratio} = (p - v)/p$; must lie between 0 and 1.
- [ ] **Profit Function:** $\pi = (p - v) \times Q - \text{FC}$
- [ ] **Margin of Safety:** $\text{MOS} = Q_{\text{actual}} - Q_{BE}$ (a negative MOS indicates projected loss).
- [ ] **Multi-Product BEP:** Use weighted CM with sales mix proportions that sum to 1.
- [ ] **Cost Classification:** Fixed Cost is constant; Variable Cost scales with output; Semi-variable must be split.
- [ ] **Assumptions to State in Exam:** Linear cost-revenue behaviour, constant price, constant sales mix, FC truly fixed, $v$ constant per unit.
- [ ] **Graph Components:** TR (through origin, slope $p$); TC (Y-intercept FC, slope $v$); FC horizontal; intersection = BEP.
- [ ] **Real-World Tie-in:** BEP is essential for NABARD bank appraisals, MSME loan assessments, and agro-pricing decisions.
- [ ] **Limitations Acknowledgement:** Static model, ignores seasonality, assumes linear cost-revenue — mention in theory answers.
- [ ] **Operational Rule of Thumb:** Higher FC → Higher BEP; Higher $p$ or Lower $v$ → Lower BEP; Adding product with positive CM → Lower overall BEP.
- [ ] **Examiner's Hot-Spot Topics:** Multi-product BEP, Margin of Safety ratio, BEP chart drawing, and discussion of BEA assumptions and limitations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application in Food Industry - Project Appraisal
Market Management Concepts - Consumer Market
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT865 - AGRO-INDUSTRIAL PROJECT PLANNING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Market Management Concepts - Consumer Market

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Consumer Market (Definition – KTU Standard)**
> A **Consumer Market** is the aggregate of all actual and potential buyers of final products and services that are intended for personal, family, or household use, and *not* for business, commercial, or industrial resale. In the agro-industrial context, the consumer market represents end-users who purchase processed agricultural goods (e.g., packaged rice, dairy products, fruit juices, bakery items) directly for consumption rather than for further processing.

In marketing management, the consumer market is studied under the broader umbrella of **Market Management**, which refers to the systematic process of analyzing, planning, implementing, and controlling marketing activities — including the **4Ps (Product, Price, Place, Promotion)** — to facilitate favourable exchange relationships with target consumers.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Mango Vendor → The Multinational Juice Brand**
> Imagine a small farmer selling fresh mangoes at the local *santhai* (Kerala market). His buyer is a **household consumer** — they buy for eating at home, not for resale. Now think of a company like *Maaza* or *Frooti*: their consumer is the same end-household, but the buying process, marketing mix, and decision journey are vastly more complex.

The consumer market is essentially the **"last stop"** in the value chain — where a product's journey ends in someone's kitchen, lunchbox, or refrigerator. Managing this final interface is what determines whether an agro-industrial venture succeeds or fails.

## 1.3 Key Distinguishing Features of Consumer Markets

| Feature | Consumer Market | Industrial/Business Market |
|---|---|---|
| Buyer Identity | Individuals / households | Firms, institutions |
| Purchase Volume | Small, frequent | Large, infrequent |
| Decision Process | Simple → complex (depending on involvement) | Multi-stakeholder, formal RFPs |
| Driver | Emotions, lifestyle, culture | Logic, ROI, technical specs |
| Geographic Spread | Widely dispersed | Regionally concentrated |
| Channel Length | Long (Manufacturer → Wholesaler → Retailer → Consumer) | Short / direct |

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Consumer Market Analysis

The following **standard marketing metrics** are universally applied in KTU-evaluated market management problems:

- **CPM (Cost Per Mille / Cost Per Thousand Impressions)** – measures ad cost efficiency.
- **CLV (Customer Lifetime Value)** – total revenue a customer generates over their lifetime.
- **CAC (Customer Acquisition Cost)** – cost to acquire one new paying customer.
- **Market Penetration Rate** = $\frac{\text{Customers Acquired}}{\text{Total Target Market}} \times 100\%$
- **Repeat Purchase Rate** = $\frac{\text{Customers with} \geq 2 \text{ purchases}}{\text{Total Customers}} \times 100\%$
- **NPS (Net Promoter Score)** = $\%\text{Promoters} - \%\text{Detractors}$ (range **−100 to +100**).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework – The 5 Pillars of Consumer Market Management

### Pillar 1: **Consumer Buying Behaviour (CBB)**

Consumer behaviour is the study of *how, why, when, and where* people make purchase decisions. Philip Kotler's classic model is the KTU benchmark.

**Major Factors Influencing CBB:**
1. **Cultural Factors** – Culture, sub-culture, social class.
2. **Social Factors** – Reference groups, family, roles & status.
3. **Personal Factors** – Age, life-cycle stage, occupation, lifestyle, economic situation.
4. **Psychological Factors** – Motivation (Maslow), perception, learning, beliefs & attitudes.

### Pillar 2: **STP Framework (Segmentation, Targeting, Positioning)**

- **Segmentation** – Dividing the heterogeneous market into homogeneous sub-groups.
  - *Demographic*: age, income, gender.
  - *Geographic*: region, climate, urban/rural.
  - *Psychographic*: lifestyle, values.
  - *Behavioural*: usage rate, loyalty, occasion.
- **Targeting** – Evaluating and selecting one or more segments.
- **Positioning** – Designing a distinctive image in the consumer's mind.

### Pillar 3: **The Marketing Mix (4Ps → 7Ps)**

| Pillar | Element | Agro-Industrial Application Example |
|---|---|---|
| **P1** | Product | Quality, packaging size, brand, organic certification |
| **P2** | Price | MRP, discounts, regional pricing |
| **P3** | Place | Distribution via super-markets, kirana, e-commerce |
| **P4** | Promotion | Ads, sales promotion, digital marketing |
| P5 (Services) | People | Sales staff, FPO trainers |
| P6 | Process | Order fulfilment, cold-chain integrity |
| P7 | Physical Evidence | Brand logo, store display, packaging |

### Pillar 4: **Consumer Decision-Making Process (5-Stage Model)**

1. **Need Recognition** – Consumer perceives a gap between desired and actual state.
2. **Information Search** – Internal (memory) and external (online, peers).
3. **Evaluation of Alternatives** – Compares brands on attributes.
4. **Purchase Decision** – Choice is made (subject to situational factors).
5. **Post-Purchase Behaviour** – Satisfaction / cognitive dissonance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Variations**: For low-involvement products (rice, salt, sugar), consumers often skip stages 2 and 3, going straight to habitual purchase. For high-involvement products (premium olive oil, organic baby food), all 5 stages are rigorously traversed.

### Pillar 5: **Market Management Functions (Marketing Management Process)**

1. **Analyzing** – SWOT, PESTEL, market research.
2. **Planning** – Marketing plan, objectives, budget.
3. **Implementing** – 4Ps deployment, sales force execution.
4. **Controlling** – Sales analysis, variance analysis, corrective action.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **high-yield formula reference** for KTU ESE numerical/analytical questions on consumer markets.

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Market Penetration Rate | $\text{MPR} = \dfrac{N_a}{N_t} \times 100\%$ | $N_a$ = acquired, $N_t$ = target |
| 2 | Market Share | $\text{MS} = \dfrac{\text{Firm Sales}}{\text{Total Market Sales}} \times 100\%$ | expressed in **%** |
| 3 | Customer Acquisition Cost | $\text{CAC} = \dfrac{\text{Total Marketing Spend}}{\text{New Customers}}$ | in **INR / customer** |
| 4 | Customer Lifetime Value (Basic) | $\text{CLV} = \text{AOV} \times \text{APF} \times \text{CLV years}$ | AOV = Avg Order Value, APF = Annual Purchase Frequency |
| 5 | Repeat Purchase Rate | $\text{RPR} = \dfrac{\text{Repeat Customers}}{\text{Total Customers}} \times 100\%$ | expressed in **%** |
| 6 | Net Promoter Score | $\text{NPS} = \%P - \%D$ | bounded in $[-100,\, +100]$ |
| 7 | Cost Per Mille | $\text{CPM} = \dfrac{\text{Ad Cost}}{\text{Impressions}} \times 1000$ | in **INR per 1000 impressions** |
| 8 | Break-Even Volume | $\text{BEV} = \dfrac{F}{P - V}$ | $F$=fixed cost, $P$=price, $V$=variable cost |
| 9 | Price Elasticity of Demand | $E_d = \dfrac{\%\Delta Q_d}{\%\Delta P}$ | magnitude interpretation in notes |
| 10 | Inventory Turnover (for place) | $\text{IT} = \dfrac{\text{COGS}}{\text{Avg Inventory}}$ | times per year |

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Agro-Industrial Engineering

For a B.Tech student specializing in agro-industrial project planning, mastering consumer market concepts is critical for:
- **Product-Market Fit (PMF)** validation before large capex.
- Designing **D2C (Direct-to-Consumer)** strategies for FPOs.
- Building **brand equity** for processed food lines.
- Negotiating shelf-space with **modern retail** chains.
- Conducting **feasibility studies** for new agro-processing units.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Calculating Market Share and CLV

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A Kerala-based agro-processing firm "SpiceLeaf Pvt. Ltd." sells organic turmeric powder. Annual firm sales = **₹1.2 crore**. Total addressable market sales for organic turmeric in Kerala = **₹8 crore**. Average Order Value (AOV) = **₹400**. Annual Purchase Frequency (APF) = **4**. Average customer relationship years = **3**.

### Step 1 — Market Share Calculation

$$\text{Market Share} = \frac{\text{Firm Sales}}{\text{Total Market Sales}} \times 100\%$$

$$\text{Market Share} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^7}{8 \times 10^7} \times 100\%$$

$$\text{Market Share} = \frac{1.2}{8} \times 100\% = 0.15 \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{Market Share} = 15\%}$$

**Interpretation:** SpiceLeaf holds **15%** of Kerala's organic turmeric market — a solid mid-tier player.

### Step 2 — Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)

$$\text{CLV} = \text{AOV} \times \text{APF} \times \text{CLV years}$$

$$\text{CLV} = 400 \times 4 \times 3$$

$$\text{CLV} = 1600 \times 3$$

$$\boxed{\text{CLV} = \text{₹}4{,}800}$$

**Interpretation:** Each customer is worth **₹4,800** over 3 years. If CAC exceeds ₹4,800, the firm loses money per customer.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Break-Even Volume for a New Agro-Product

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A startup plans to launch cold-pressed coconut oil. Fixed costs = **₹6,00,000** per month. Selling price per litre = **₹350**. Variable cost per litre = **₹220**. Find the break-even volume and interpret.

### Step 1 — Identify Parameters
- $F = 6{,}00{,}000$ (Fixed Cost, INR/month)
- $P = 350$ (Price, INR/litre)
- $V = 220$ (Variable Cost, INR/litre)

### Step 2 — Apply Break-Even Formula

$$\text{BEV} = \frac{F}{P - V}$$

$$\text{BEV} = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{350 - 220}$$

$$\text{BEV} = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{130}$$

$$\boxed{\text{BEV} \approx 4{,}615 \text{ litres / month}}$$

### Step 3 — Interpretation
- Below **4,615 litres/month**, the firm operates at a loss.
- Above this volume, every additional litre contributes **₹130** (contribution margin) towards profit.
- *Strategic Insight:* The startup must achieve ~154 litres/day — feasible with 3–4 retail outlets in Kochi city.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — NPS Calculation and Strategic Implication

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A dairy brand surveys 1,000 consumers. 600 are "Promoters" (9 or 10 score), 300 are "Passives" (7–8), and 100 are "Detractors" (0–6).

### Step 1 — Compute % Promoters and % Detractors

$$\%P = \frac{600}{1000} \times 100\% = 60\%$$

$$\%D = \frac{100}{1000} \times 100\% = 10\%$$

### Step 2 — Compute NPS

$$\text{NPS} = \%P - \%D = 60 - 10$$

$$\boxed{\text{NPS} = +50}$$

**Interpretation:** An NPS of **+50** is **world-class** (anything above +50 is excellent). The brand has strong word-of-mouth potential and can leverage referral marketing.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing a Target Segment

> [!NOTE]
> The following is a **KTU-style analytical matrix** typically asked in 7-mark sub-questions. It evaluates three potential consumer segments for a new organic mango pulp brand.

| Evaluation Criterion | Weight $w_i$ | Urban Premium (Score 1–10) | Tier-2 Mass (Score 1–10) | Export (NRIs) (Score 1–10) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Market Size (Volume) | 0.20 | 7 | 9 | 5 |
| Willingness to Pay | 0.25 | 9 | 5 | 10 |
| Distribution Ease | 0.20 | 9 | 8 | 4 |
| Brand Loyalty Potential | 0.20 | 7 | 6 | 8 |
| Competitive Intensity | 0.15 | 4 | 7 | 6 |
| **Weighted Score** | **1.00** | **7.40** | **7.00** | **6.70** |

$$\text{WS}_{\text{Urban Premium}} = (0.20 \times 7) + (0.25 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 7) + (0.15 \times 4)$$

$$= 1.40 + 2.25 + 1.80 + 1.40 + 0.60 = 7.45$$

$$\text{WS}_{\text{Tier-2 Mass}} = (0.20 \times 9) + (0.25 \times 5) + (0.20 \times 8) + (0.20 \times 6) + (0.15 \times 7) = 1.80 + 1.25 + 1.60 + 1.20 + 1.05 = 6.90$$

$$\text{WS}_{\text{NRI Export}} = (0.20 \times 5) + (0.25 \times 10) + (0.20 \times 4) + (0.20 \times 8) + (0.15 \times 6) = 1.00 + 2.50 + 0.80 + 1.60 + 0.90 = 6.80$$

**Decision:** **Urban Premium** is the recommended primary target segment (highest weighted score ≈ **7.45**).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Consumer Decision-Making Process (5-Stage Kotler Model)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Node1: Need Recognition] --> B[Node2: Information Search]
    B --> C[Node3: Evaluation of Alternatives]
    C --> D[Node4: Purchase Decision]
    D --> E[Node5: Post-Purchase Behaviour]
    E -->|Satisfied| F[Node6: Brand Loyalty Loop]
    E -->|Dissatisfied| G[Node7: Cognitive Dissonance Negative WOM]
    F --> A
    G --> H[Node8: Brand Switching Exit]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** This flowchart maps the **5-stage consumer decision model**. The dashed-style loops (`F → A`) capture the loyalty cycle, while the dissonance branch (`G → H`) is critical for post-purchase service design in agro-brands (e.g., complaint redressal for adulteration).

## 4.2 STP Framework Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1[StepS1: Market Segmentation] --> S2[StepS2: Market Targeting]
    S2 --> S3[StepS3: Market Positioning]
    S1 --> D1[Demographic]
    S1 --> D2[Geographic]
    S1 --> D3[Psychographic]
    S1 --> D4[Behavioural]
    S2 --> T1[Undifferentiated]
    S2 --> T2[Differentiated]
    S2 --> T3[Concentrated Niche]
    S2 --> T4[Micromarketing]
    S3 --> P1[Value Proposition Design]
    S3 --> P2[Brand Identity Creation]
    S3 --> P3[Communication Mix Selection]
```

## 4.3 Marketing Mix (7Ps) — Agro-Industrial Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MM[NodeMM: Marketing Mix 7Ps] --> P1[NodeP1: Product]
    MM --> P2[NodeP2: Price]
    MM --> P3[NodeP3: Place]
    MM --> P4[NodeP4: Promotion]
    MM --> P5[NodeP5: People]
    MM --> P6[NodeP6: Process]
    MM --> P7[NodeP7: Physical Evidence]
    P1 --> P1A[Quality, Packaging, Brand, Certification]
    P2 --> P2A[MRP, Discount, Regional Pricing]
    P3 --> P3A[Modern Retail, Kirana, E Commerce]
    P4 --> P4A[Ads, Sales Promo, Digital]
    P5 --> P5A[Sales Staff, FPO Members]
    P6 --> P6A[Order Fulfillment, Cold Chain]
    P7 --> P7A[Logo, Packaging, Store Display]
```

## 4.4 Market Management Process (Analyzing → Controlling)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AP[PhaseA: Analyzing] --> BP[PhaseB: Planning]
    BP --> CP[PhaseC: Implementing]
    CP --> DP[PhaseD: Controlling]
    DP -->|Feedback Loop| AP
    AP --> A1[Market Research]
    AP --> A2[SWOT PESTEL]
    BP --> B1[Marketing Objectives]
    BP --> B2[Budget Allocation]
    CP --> C1[4Ps Deployment]
    CP --> C2[Sales Force Action]
    DP --> D1[Sales Variance Analysis]
    DP --> D2[Corrective Action]
```

## 4.5 Consumer Behaviour Influence Wheel (Adapted for Agro-Products)

```mermaid
graph TD
    CB[Center: Consumer Buying Decision] --> F1[F1: Cultural]
    CB --> F2[F2: Social]
    CB --> F3[F3: Personal]
    CB --> F4[F4: Psychological]
    F1 --> F1A[Culture Sub Culture Social Class]
    F2 --> F2A[Reference Group Family Role Status]
    F3 --> F3A[Age Lifestyle Income Occupation]
    F4 --> F4A[Maslow Motivation Perception Learning]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the term "Consumer Market" and state any two of its distinguishing characteristics.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A **Consumer Market** is the aggregate of all actual and potential buyers of products and services that are intended for personal, family, or household non-business use. (1 mark)
>
> **Two distinguishing characteristics:** (2 marks, 1 each)
> 1. **Large and dispersed customer base** — millions of individual buyers spread across urban and rural geographies.
> 2. **Emotion-driven decisions** — purchase choices are influenced by lifestyle, culture, and psychological factors rather than purely rational criteria.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is "Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)"? Why is it important for an agro-industrial brand?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **CLV** is the total revenue a business can expect from a single customer account over the entire duration of their relationship with the brand. (1 mark)
>
> **Importance for agro-industrial brand:** (2 marks)
> 1. It helps allocate marketing spend efficiently — high-CLV segments justify higher CAC.
> 2. For seasonal products (e.g., mango pulp, festive sweets), CLV captures repeat-season revenue, which is critical for capacity planning.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]**

**(a) Explain the **5-stage consumer decision-making process** proposed by Philip Kotler. Apply each stage to the purchase of a **premium organic honey** brand by an urban Indian consumer. (7 marks)**

> **Step-by-Step Model Solution:**
>
> **Stage 1 — Need Recognition (1.5 marks)**
> The consumer becomes aware of a gap between the desired and actual state. *Application:* A health-conscious urban professional reads about immunity-boosting foods during the monsoon season and recognizes a need for natural sweeteners to replace refined sugar.
>
> **Stage 2 — Information Search (1.5 marks)**
> The consumer seeks information from internal memory (past experience with honey brands) and external sources (Google reviews, Instagram reels, peer recommendations, organic store visits). *Application:* She searches "best organic honey India" on Google, shortlists 3 brands, and checks FSSAI certifications.
>
> **Stage 3 — Evaluation of Alternatives (1.5 marks)**
> Alternatives are compared on attributes like price, brand reputation, packaging, certifications, and origin. *Application:* Brand A is cheaper but uncertified; Brand B is mid-priced with India Organic seal; Brand C is premium-priced with lab-test reports. The consumer builds an evoked set of attributes.
>
> **Stage 4 — Purchase Decision (1.5 marks)**
> The actual choice is made, modified by situational factors (stock availability, discount offers, peer pressure). *Application:* Brand B is selected because of a 15% festive discount and a friend's strong recommendation.
>
> **Stage 5 — Post-Purchase Behaviour (1 mark)**
> Consumer experiences satisfaction or cognitive dissonance. *Application:* After 2 weeks, the consumer perceives improved taste and energy levels → satisfaction → repeat purchase + positive word-of-mouth. *(Note: any reasonable post-purchase scenario is acceptable.)*

**(b) Discuss the **7Ps of Marketing Mix** in detail, with specific reference to the launch of a **cold-pressed virgin coconut oil** product in the Kerala consumer market. (7 marks)**

> **Step-by-Step Model Solution:**
>
> **P1 — Product (1 mark):**
> *Definition + Application.* Product is the tangible offering — design, quality, features, packaging, brand. For cold-pressed coconut oil, key elements include: 100% organic certification, cold-press technology, glass bottle packaging (premium perception), and "100% natural" label claim.
>
> **P2 — Price (1 mark):**
> *Definition + Application.* Price is the monetary value charged. Strategies include premium pricing (₹450–₹600/litre for cold-pressed), penetration pricing for volume launch, psychological pricing (₹499), and regional pricing variations between Kerala and metro markets.
>
> **P3 — Place (1 mark):**
> *Definition + Application.* Place covers distribution channels and coverage. For Kerala: super-markets (Lulu, Reliance Smart), organic stores, kirana networks, e-commerce (Amazon Fresh, BigBasket), and direct-to-consumer (brand website).
>
> **P4 — Promotion (1 mark):**
> *Definition + Application.* Promotion includes advertising, sales promotion, PR, and direct marketing. For the coconut oil launch: digital ads on YouTube/Instagram, in-store sampling, influencer tie-ups with Kerala food bloggers, free samples with newspapers.
>
> **P5 — People (1 mark):**
> *Definition + Application.* People refers to all human actors who deliver the service experience. Includes trained sales staff, FPO (Farmer Producer Organisation) trainers, customer-care executives handling queries about authenticity.
>
> **P6 — Process (1 mark):**
> *Definition + Application.* Process is the workflow of service delivery. For a cold-pressed oil: order → cold storage → packaging → quality check → dispatch (24-hour SLA) → doorstep delivery. Streamlined process builds trust.
>
> **P7 — Physical Evidence (1 mark):**
> *Definition + Application.* Physical evidence is the tangible cues that signal service quality. Includes the brand logo, premium glass bottle, tamper-proof seal, certificate of analysis inside the box, brand website aesthetics.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[Alternative to Question A, Set B]*

**(a) Define **Market Segmentation**. Explain the four bases of segmentation with **suitable agro-industrial examples** for each. (7 marks)**

> **Step-by-Step Model Solution:**
>
> **Definition (1 mark):**
> Market segmentation is the process of dividing a broad consumer market into subgroups of consumers with common needs, characteristics, or behaviours, who are likely to respond similarly to marketing actions.
>
> **Base 1 — Demographic Segmentation (1.5 marks):**
> Divides market on age, income, gender, education. *Agro-Example:* A baby-food brand (e.g., Cerelac) targets mothers aged 25–35 with infants aged 6–24 months, in the upper-middle income bracket (₹8L+).
>
> **Base 2 — Geographic Segmentation (1.5 marks):**
> Divides by region, climate, urban/rural. *Agro-Example:* A spice brand sells garam-masala blends customized to Kerala (coconut-flavoured), Punjab (richer, dairy-based), and Tamil Nadu (pepper-forward) markets.
>
> **Base 3 — Psychographic Segmentation (1.5 marks):**
> Divides by lifestyle, values, personality. *Agro-Example:* A premium organic honey brand targets "wellness enthusiasts" and "Ayurveda followers" — values-driven buyers willing to pay 2–3x the conventional price.
>
> **Base 4 — Behavioural Segmentation (1.5 marks):**
> Divides by usage rate, loyalty, occasion. *Agro-Example:* A coffee brand markets a "5 a.m. office brew" SKU for high-frequency daily drinkers, and a "Sunday family gathering" premium SKU for occasional buyers.

**(b) A Kerala-based FPO plans to launch a **premium branded mango pulp** in the domestic urban market. Using the **STP framework**, propose a complete marketing strategy. Justify each step. (7 marks)**

> **Step-by-Step Model Solution:**
>
> **Step 1 — Segmentation (2 marks):**
> The market is segmented on a combination of demographic, psychographic, and behavioural bases. Sub-segments identified: (a) Urban Premium (age 25–40, income > ₹10L, health-conscious), (b) Tier-2 Mass (price-sensitive, value-driven), (c) HoReCa (Hotels, Restaurants, Cafés — bulk institutional).
>
> **Step 2 — Targeting (2.5 marks):**
> Using the weighted-score analytical matrix (as shown in Section 3.4 above), the **Urban Premium** segment scores highest (≈7.45) on combined criteria of willingness-to-pay, distribution ease, and brand-loyalty potential. The FPO selects Urban Premium as the primary target, with HoReCa as a secondary segment.
>
> **Step 3 — Positioning (2.5 marks):**
> The product is positioned as **"100% Kerala-Made, Single-Origin, No-Preservative Mango Pulp"** — a value proposition of authenticity, premium sourcing, and clean-label integrity. The positioning statement is crafted as:
>
> *"For health-conscious urban Indian families who value authenticity, [Brand] is the premium single-origin mango pulp that delivers the true taste of Kerala's Alphonso mangoes — without any artificial additives, because we believe food should be as pure as the soil it comes from."*
>
> The 4Ps deployment follows: premium glass-jar packaging, ₹299 price point, distribution via BigBasket + Lulu + brand D2C website, and digital-led promotion targeting Instagram wellness influencers.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Consumer Market Questions:**
> 1. **Generic 4P answer without agro-context (–2 to –3 marks):** Students often write textbook definitions of the 4Ps without applying them to an agro-product. Examiners *require* domain-specific application.
> 2. **Skipping the quantitative part in STP questions (–1 mark):** Whenever a question asks you to choose a target segment, you must back it with a **weighted score matrix or scoring rubric** — not just narrative preference.
> 3. **Confusing 4Ps with 7Ps (–0.5 mark):** For service-dominant agro-products (e.g., subscription-based fresh-milk delivery), 7Ps is mandatory. For packaged FMCG, 4Ps suffices.
> 4. **Forgetting the 'post-purchase' stage (–1 mark):** Many students stop at "purchase decision." The 5th stage — post-purchase behaviour — is the differentiator between a 12-mark and 14-mark answer.
> 5. **Unit errors in CLV/CAC/NPS formulas (–0.5 mark):** Always state units explicitly: "₹ per customer," "score in [−100, +100]," etc.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Consumer Market (Module 3):**
>
> - [x] **Definition**: Consumer market = end-household buyers; non-business purchase intent.
> - [x] **Buyer Behaviour Model**: Cultural → Social → Personal → Psychological (Kotler 4-factor wheel).
> - [x] **5-Stage Decision Process**: Need → Search → Evaluate → Decide → Post-Purchase.
> - [x] **STP Framework**: Segment (4 bases) → Target (3 strategies) → Position (value prop).
> - [x] **Marketing Mix**: 4Ps (Product, Price, Place, Promotion) → 7Ps (adds People, Process, Physical Evidence).
> - [x] **Market Management Process**: Analyzing → Planning → Implementing → Controlling.
> - [x] **Key Formulas**: Market Share, CLV, CAC, NPS, BEV, Price Elasticity — must be memorised with units.
> - [x] **Agro-Context Mandate**: Every answer must use *agricultural / processed-food* examples (rice, dairy, spices, oils, fruit pulp) — not generic consumer durables.
> - [x] **Quantitative Backing**: STP targeting *must* use a weighted scoring matrix for full marks.
> - [x] **D2C and Modern Retail Trends**: Mention e-commerce platforms (BigBasket, Amazon Fresh) and D2C brand websites — current KTU-relevant keywords.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Topic Notes — Market Management Concepts: Consumer Market**
> These notes are aligned with the KTU 2024 Scheme assessment pattern, covering all five required components (Definition, Theory, Derivations, Diagrams, and Question Bank). Use the formula sheet and decision matrix in Sections 2 and 3 as quick reference tools during exam preparation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Business Market
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT865 - AGRO-INDUSTRIAL PROJECT PLANNING AND MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Business Market # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Definition of Business Market (KTU 2024 Standard) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Academic Definition (KTU Board Examiner Standard):** > The **Business Market** (also called the **Organizational Market**, **Industrial Market**, or **B2B Market**) refers to the market structure comprising all individuals, organizations, and institutional buyers who purchase **goods and services** for the purpose of **resale, direct use in production of other goods/services, or general operational use** rather than for personal, family, or household consumption. In the agro-industrial context, the business market includes **farmers, agri-input dealers, food processing companies, cold chain operators, wholesalers, retailers, exporters, and government procurement agencies** who transact in agricultural inputs (seeds, fertilizers, machinery) and outputs (cereals, dairy, processed foods). --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Hook) > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Restaurant Supply Chain":** > Imagine a kitchen in your home. *You* are a **consumer** — you buy rice to eat. But the **hotel restaurant down the street** also buys rice, but **not to eat personally** — it buys 500 kg/day **to cook meals and sell to its customers**. > > The hotel is a **business buyer**. It cares about: > - **Bulk pricing** (price per quintal, not per kg) > - **Consistent quality** (same grain size every batch) > - **Delivery schedules** (must arrive at 6 AM sharp) > - **Credit terms** (will pay in 30 days) > > The **Business Market** is simply the marketplace where *these kinds of institutional/organizational transactions* happen — large-volume, relationship-driven, and professionally negotiated. --- ## 1.3 Key Characteristics of Business Markets (Syllabus Highlight) The business market differs structurally from consumer markets across **eight core dimensions**: | # | Characteristic | Business Market Reality | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Demand Nature** | **Derived Demand** — depends on consumer demand for end-products | | 2 | **Buyer Profile** | Fewer, larger, geographically concentrated buyers | | 3 | **Purchase Volume** | Very high order quantities (bulk) | | 4 | **Decision Process** | **Multi-person buying centers** (formal procurement) | | 5 | **Relationship Type** | Long-term partnerships and supply contracts | | 6 | **Negotiation** | Direct bargaining, technical specifications, contracts | | 7 | **Price Sensitivity** | Highly price-sensitive due to margin pressures | | 8 | **Reciprocity** | Buyers often sell back (e.g., contract farming) | > [!TIP] > **KTU Memory Anchor:** *"Fewer Buyers, Bigger Orders, Smarter Negotiators."* --- ## 1.4 Standard Classification Metrics (Bold Constants) > [!IMPORTANT] > The KTU 2024 syllabus identifies **four core types** of business markets: > > - **Industrial/Producer Market** — buys for production use (e.g., a flour mill buying wheat) > - **Reseller Market** — buys for resale (wholesalers, retailers, distributors) > - **Government/Institutional Market** — buys for public service (FCI, PDS, co-operatives) > - **Agro-Service Market** — buys services (cold storage, logistics, agro-tourism providers) > > **Reference Volume Metric:** Globally, **B2B transactions are estimated at 2–3× the volume of B2C markets** in developed economies. # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Structural Breakdown of the Business Market Ecosystem The business market operates on a **chain-of-transformation logic** — every transaction adds value: ### Step 1: **Input Suppliers → Producers** - Suppliers of seeds, fertilizers, agrochemicals, and farm machinery sell to farmers/producer firms. - Transaction basis: **technical specifications, bulk pricing, credit terms**. ### Step 2: **Producers → Processors/Agro-Industries** - Farmers and producer cooperatives sell raw produce to food processing units. - Example: Mango growers → pulp extraction factories → juice brands. ### Step 3: **Processors → Wholesalers/Distributors** - Processed goods flow into the distribution network via C&F agents, stockists, and distributors. ### Step 4: **Wholesalers → Retailers → Final Business Buyer** - Retail chains, HoReCa (Hotels, Restaurants, Catering), and institutional buyers procure in bulk. ### Step 5: **Government Procurement Layer** - FCI, NAFED, State Marketing Federations, and PDS networks act as parallel institutional buyers. > [!NOTE] > **"Why" Logic:** Every layer in the business market adds **value, time, place, and possession utility**. This is why B2B marketing is *system-oriented*, not *emotion-oriented* like consumer marketing. --- ## 2.2 Core Theoretical Framework: The Derived Demand Principle > [!IMPORTANT] > **DERIVED DEMAND — KTU Most Tested Concept:** > The demand for a business product is **NOT direct** — it is **derived from the demand for the downstream consumer product**. > > $$D_{\text{business}} = f(D_{\text{consumer}})$$ ### Mathematical Representation: If a bakery expects consumer bread demand $Q_c$, then flour demand $Q_f$ follows as: $$Q_f = \alpha \cdot Q_c$$ where **$\alpha$** is the **input-output coefficient** (kg flour required per kg bread). If consumer demand for bread rises by **$\Delta Q_c$**, business demand for flour rises by: $$\Delta Q_f = \alpha \cdot \Delta Q_c$$ ### Real-World Agro Example: - **Consumer Demand Rise:** Urban Indian demand for frozen peas ↑ by 10% - **Derived Demand Effect:** Demand for IQF (Individual Quick Frozen) processing machinery ↑, demand for cold storage leasing ↑, demand for pea seed varieties ↑ — *cascading effect across the business market chain.* --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Unit / Notation | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Derived Demand** | $D_b = f(D_c)$ | $D_b$: Business demand; $D_c$: Consumer demand | | 2 | **Input-Output Coefficient** | $\alpha = \dfrac{Q_f}{Q_c}$ | Dimensionless ratio | | 3 | **Price Elasticity (Business)** | $E_p = \dfrac{\%\Delta Q_b}{\%\Delta P_b}$ | Sign convention: negative for normal goods | | 4 | **Joint Demand** | $Q_{\text{output}} = f(X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n)$ | Multiple complementary inputs | | 5 | **Market Concentration (CR4)** | $CR_4 = \sum_{i=1}^{4} S_i$ | $S_i$: market share of top 4 firms | | 6 | **Buying Center Influence Weight** | $W_i = \dfrac{D_i}{\sum D_j}$ | $D_i$: decisional power of member $i$ | | 7 | **Order Quantity (EOQ)** | $EOQ = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | $D$: demand, $S$: order cost, $H$: holding cost | | 8 | **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)** | $CLV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{(R_t - C_t)}{(1+r)^t}$ | $R$: revenue, $C$: cost, $r$: discount rate | > [!WARNING] > **Markdown Syntax Notice:** All vertical bars in formulas above use `\vert` or mathematical notation — *never raw pipes* inside table rows to prevent syntax breakage. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Agro-Industries - **Procurement Planning:** Agri-businesses use **derived demand models** to forecast raw material needs. - **Capacity Planning:** Cold chain operators compute **EOQ** for inventory of perishables. - **Buyer Targeting:** B2B marketers compute **CR4 (4-firm concentration ratio)** to identify market structure (perfect competition, oligopoly). - **Relationship Marketing:** Firms calculate **CLV** to prioritize high-value institutional accounts (e.g., ITC e-Choupal contract farmers). # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation: Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) — KTU Board Favorite The EOQ model minimizes **total inventory cost** = ordering cost + holding cost. ### Step 1: Define Cost Components - **Annual Demand:** $D$ (units/year) - **Ordering Cost per Order:** $S$ (₹/order) - **Holding Cost per Unit per Year:** $H$ (₹/unit/year) - **Order Quantity:** $Q$ (units) ### Step 2: Express Number of Orders per Year $$\text{Number of Orders} = \frac{D}{Q}$$ ### Step 3: Total Ordering Cost (TOC) $$\text{TOC} = \frac{D}{Q} \cdot S$$ ### Step 4: Average Inventory and Total Holding Cost (THC) $$\text{Average Inventory} = \frac{Q}{2}$$ $$\text{THC} = \frac{Q}{2} \cdot H$$ ### Step 5: Total Cost Function (TC) $$TC(Q) = \frac{DS}{Q} + \frac{HQ}{2}$$ ### Step 6: Differentiate TC with respect to Q and Set to Zero $$\frac{d(TC)}{dQ} = -\frac{DS}{Q^2} + \frac{H}{2} = 0$$ ### Step 7: Solve for Q $$\frac{H}{2} = \frac{DS}{Q^2}$$ $$Q^2 = \frac{2DS}{H}$$ $$Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Result — The Harris-Wilson EOQ Formula:** > > $$EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}$$ ### Step 8: Verification (Second-Order Condition) $$\frac{d^2(TC)}{dQ^2} = \frac{2DS}{Q^3} > 0$$ This confirms a **minimum** (positive second derivative → convex function). --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style) > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** An agro-processing firm demands **10,000 tonnes/year** of sugar cane. Ordering cost is **₹5,000/order**, and holding cost is **₹200/tonne/year**. Find the EOQ and total minimum cost. ### Solution: **Step 1:** Identify parameters - $D = 10{,}000$ tonnes - $S = ₹5{,}000$ - $H = ₹200$ **Step 2:** Apply EOQ formula $$EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10{,}000 \times 5{,}000}{200}}$$ **Step 3:** Simplify numerator $$EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{100{,}000{,}000}{200}}$$ **Step 4:** Compute division $$EOQ = \sqrt{500{,}000}$$ **Step 5:** Take square root $$EOQ \approx 707.1 \text{ tonnes}$$ **Step 6:** Compute Minimum Total Cost $$TC = \frac{(10{,}000)(5{,}000)}{707.1} + \frac{(200)(707.1)}{2}$$ **Step 7:** Evaluate each term $$TC = 70{,}710 + 70{,}710$$ **Step 8:** Final Answer $$TC_{\min} \approx ₹1{,}41{,}420$$ > [!TIP] > **Examiner Tip:** Always show units in the final answer and verify with the second-order condition. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Consumer Market vs. Business Market | Parameter | Consumer Market (B2C) | Business Market (B2B) | |---|---|---| | Buyer Count | Millions | Few hundreds/thousands | | Purchase Volume | Small | Very large | | Geographic Spread | Dispersed | Concentrated | | Decision Maker | Individual | Buying Center | | Demand Nature | Direct | Derived | | Negotiation Style | Fixed price/display | Bargaining/contracts | | Promotion Focus | Mass media advertising | Personal selling + trade shows | | Relationship | Transactional | Long-term partnerships | | Technical Specification | Standardized | Customized | | Payment Terms | Cash/credit card | Credit lines, advance, installments | | Risk Profile | Low per transaction | High per transaction | # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Business Market Flow in Agro-Industry ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Input Suppliers
Seeds, Fertilizers, Machinery] --> B[Primary Producers
Farmers, Plantations] B --> C[Agro Processors
Mills, Dairies, Canneries] C --> D[Wholesalers and Distributors
Stockists, C and F Agents] D --> E[Retailers and HoReCa
Hotels, Restaurants, Catering] E --> F[End Consumers
Households, Export Markets] G[Government Procurement
FCI, NAFED, PDS] -.-> C G -.-> B H[Institutional Buyers
Defence, Schools, Hospitals] -.-> E I[Service Providers
Cold Chain, Logistics, Insurance] -.-> C I -.-> D ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** Solid arrows (→) show primary commercial flow. Dotted arrows (-.->) show parallel institutional and service flows intersecting the main chain. --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Buying Center Decision Structure ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph BC[Buying Center at a Food Processor] U1[Users
Production Staff] --> DEC I1[Influencers
Quality Managers] --> DEC B1[Buyers
Procurement Officers] --> DEC D1[Deciders
Plant Head / MD] --> DEC G1[Gatekeepers
Reception / Tech Screening] --> DEC end DEC --> V[Final Purchase Decision] V --> S[Supplier Selection and Contract] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Insight:** The Buying Center is **not a permanent organizational unit** — it is a *temporary task group* formed for each major purchase decision. The composition changes with purchase value and complexity. --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Derived Demand Cascade ```mermaid flowchart TD DC[Consumer Demand Rise
e.g., +10 percent packaged rice] --> DF[Derived Demand
Rice Mills expand capacity] DF --> DM1[Machinery Demand
Paddy processing equipment] DF --> DM2[Input Demand
Paddy seed varieties] DF --> DM3[Service Demand
Storage, transport, finance] DF --> DM4[Packaging Demand
BOPP bags, jute sacks] ``` --- ## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture (KTU Examiner Reference) > [!NOTE] > Since this is a non-physical systems topic, the following block diagram maps the **functional architecture of a B2B marketing decision** in agro-industries: ```mermaid flowchart TD MA[Market Analysis Layer
CR4, Segmentation, Sizing] --> BG[Buyer Behavior Layer
Derived Demand, Buying Center] BG --> MS[Marketing Strategy Layer
Product, Price, Place, Promotion] MS --> RM[Relationship Management Layer
CLV, CRM, Contracts] RM --> FE[Feedback and Learning
Continuous Improvement Loop] FE --> MA ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP --- ## 📝 PART A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions ### **Question 1: Define Business Market. List any four characteristics.** *(L1 — Remember)* **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]** **Model Answer:** The **Business Market** is the marketplace where organizations purchase goods and services for use in production, operations, or resale — *not for personal consumption*. **Four Characteristics:** 1. **Fewer but larger buyers** concentrated geographically. 2. **Derived demand** — depends on downstream consumer demand. 3. **Multi-person decision-making** through formal buying centers. 4. **Long-term relationship orientation** with negotiated contracts. *(1 + 2 marks = 3 marks)* --- ### **Question 2: What is Derived Demand? Give one agro-industrial example.** *(L2 — Understand)* **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** **Model Answer:** **Derived Demand** is the demand for a business product that arises *because of* demand for the consumer product it helps produce. $$D_{\text{business}} = f(D_{\text{consumer}})$$ **Agro-Industrial Example:** When urban consumer demand for **packaged fruit juice** rises, the *derived demand* for **fruit pulp processing equipment** and **tetra-pack machinery** rises correspondingly. *(1 + 2 marks = 3 marks)* --- ## 📝 PART B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks):** *(L3 — Apply / L4 — Analyze)* > **[KTU University Exam — July 2024 Model]** > > **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain in detail the **structural differences between Consumer Market and Business Market** with suitable agro-industrial examples. > > **(b)** [7 Marks] An agro-input dealer sells fertilizer with annual demand **$D = 8{,}000$ bags/year**, ordering cost **$S = ₹2{,}000$/order**, and holding cost **$H = ₹100$/bag/year**. > > Calculate the **EOQ** and **Minimum Total Inventory Cost** using standard assumptions. --- #### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks] **Introduction (1 mark):** The Consumer Market (B2C) involves transactions for *personal use*, whereas the Business Market (B2B) involves transactions for *production, operations, or resale*. **Structural Differences (5 marks — tabular form preferred by KTU examiners):** | Parameter | Consumer Market | Business Market | |---|---|---| | **Buyers** | Millions, dispersed | Few, concentrated | | **Volume per transaction** | Small | Large | | **Decision-making** | Individual buyer | Buying Center | | **Demand Nature** | Direct | Derived | | **Relationship** | Transactional | Long-term partnership | **Agro-Industrial Examples (1 mark):** - *Consumer:* A household buying 1 kg of atta from a retail store. - *Business:* An ITC factory buying 500 tonnes of wheat from FCI mandi auctions. --- #### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks] **Step 1 — Identify parameters (1 mark):** - $D = 8{,}000$ bags - $S = ₹2{,}000$/order - $H = ₹100$/bag/year **Step 2 — State EOQ formula (1 mark):** $$EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute values (1 mark):** $$EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 8{,}000 \times 2{,}000}{100}}$$ **Step 4 — Compute numerator (1 mark):** $$EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{32{,}000{,}000}{100}}$$ **Step 5 — Final EOQ (1 mark):** $$EOQ = \sqrt{320{,}000} \approx 565.7 \text{ bags}$$ **Step 6 — Compute Minimum Total Cost (1 mark):** $$TC_{\min} = \frac{DS}{EOQ} + \frac{H \cdot EOQ}{2} = \frac{8{,}000 \times 2{,}000}{565.7} + \frac{100 \times 565.7}{2}$$ **Step 7 — Final Answer (1 mark):** $$TC_{\min} \approx ₹28{,}284 + ₹28{,}285 = ₹56{,}569$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks):** *(L3 — Apply / L4 — Analyze)* > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question]** > > **(a)** [7 Marks] Discuss the concept of **Buying Center** in business markets. Explain the **five roles** with examples from the agro-processing industry. > > **(b)** [7 Marks] A poultry feed manufacturer purchases maize in bulk. Annual demand is **$D = 12{,}000$ quintals**, ordering cost **$S = ₹3{,}000$/order**, holding cost **$H = ₹150$/quintal/year**. > > Find the **EOQ**, **number of orders per year**, and **time between orders** if the firm operates **300 days/year**. --- #### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks] **Concept (2 marks):** A **Buying Center** is the *informal, cross-functional group* of organizational members who participate in a major purchase decision. It is *not a permanent department* — it forms per purchase situation. **Five Roles (4 marks — 1 mark each minimum):** 1. **Users** — Production line workers who actually use maize in feed formulation. 2. **Influencers** — Quality control and R&D team setting nutritional specifications. 3. **Buyers** — Procurement officers who negotiate with maize suppliers. 4. **Deciders** — Plant Manager or MD who approves the final vendor. 5. **Gatekeepers** — Reception/technical screening staff controlling supplier access. **Agro-Processing Example (1 mark):** A dairy cooperative's Buying Center for bulk procurement of cattle feed includes *veterinarians* (influencers), *procurement managers* (buyers), *dairy plant head* (decider), and *receptionists* (gatekeepers). --- #### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks] **Step 1 — Parameters (1 mark):** - $D = 12{,}000$ quintals - $S = ₹3{,}000$ - $H = ₹150$ - Operating days $= 300$ **Step 2 — EOQ (1.5 marks):** $$EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 12{,}000 \times 3{,}000}{150}} = \sqrt{480{,}000} \approx 692.8 \text{ quintals}$$ **Step 3 — Number of Orders/Year (1.5 marks):** $$N = \frac{D}{EOQ} = \frac{12{,}000}{692.8} \approx 17.32 \text{ orders/year}$$ **Step 4 — Days Between Orders (TBO) (1.5 marks):** $$TBO = \frac{300}{17.32} \approx 17.32 \text{ days/order}$$ **Step 5 — Interpretation (1.5 marks):** The firm should place approximately **17 orders/year**, one every **17 days**, of about **693 quintals** each to minimize total inventory cost. --- ## ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps Identified by KTU Board Examiners:** > > 1. **Confusing "Derived Demand" with "Direct Demand":** Many students write that the *consumer* creates derived demand — *WRONG*. It is the **business product** whose demand is derived. > 2. **Forgetting Units in EOQ Final Answer:** Always write *"≈ 565.7 bags"* not just "565.7". Examiner awards 0.5 marks for correct unit mention. > 3. **Mixing B2C and B2B Examples:** If the question specifies *agro-industrial*, do NOT give e-commerce/consumer-tech examples. Use *seeds, tractors, cold chain* as anchors. > 4. **Skipping Step Notation in Derivations:** Showing the formula substitution without intermediate algebraic steps loses **2 marks** under KTU valuation norms. > 5. **Not Mentioning Buying Center as "Informal":** Writing it as a *formal department* is a common conceptual error costing **1 mark** in 7-mark questions. > 6. **Missing Two-Digit Decimal in EOQ:** KTU expects at least **one decimal place** accuracy (e.g., 707.1 not 707). --- ## 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST > [!TIP] > **High-Density Summary for Last-Minute Revision:** - ✅ **Business Market = B2B** — institutional buyers purchasing for production, operations, or resale. - ✅ **Four Types:** Industrial, Reseller, Government/Institutional, Agro-Service. - ✅ **Derived Demand:** $D_b = f(D_c)$ — business demand flows *from* consumer demand. - ✅ **Input-Output Coefficient:** $\alpha = Q_f / Q_c$ — quantifies derived demand strength. - ✅ **Eight Key Characteristics:** Derived demand, fewer buyers, bulk volume, multi-person decisions, long-term relationships, negotiation, reciprocity, technical specs. - ✅ **Buying Center Roles:** Users, Influencers, Buyers, Deciders, Gatekeepers (UI-BDG mnemonic). - ✅ **EOQ Formula:** $Q^* = \sqrt{2DS/H}$ — minimizes TOC + THC. - ✅ **Customer Lifetime Value:** $CLV = \sum (R_t - C_t)/(1+r)^t$ — prioritizes high-value accounts. - ✅ **4-Firm Concentration Ratio:** $CR_4 = \sum_{i=1}^{4} S_i$ — measures market structure. - ✅ **Joint Demand:** Multiple complementary inputs required simultaneously (e.g., seeds + fertilizer + pesticide). - ✅ **Agro-Industrial Examples:** ITC e-Choupal, Amul cooperative, FCI MSP procurement, NAFED buffer stocking. - ✅ **KTU Examiner Loves:** Derived demand cascade, EOQ derivation steps, buying center roles table. - ✅ **Mnemonic Anchor:** *"Few Buyers, Big Orders, Long Contracts"* (FB-BO-LC). ---

topics.title: Marketing Environment - Market Segmentation, Market Measurement and Forecasting, Advertisement, Publicity, Market Information System, Market Research, Management of Distribution Channel
Factors Influencing Consumer Buying Behaviour, Export Trade - Government Regulations
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT865 - AGRO-INDUSTRIAL PROJECT PLANNING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Factors Influencing Consumer Buying Behaviour, Export Trade - Government Regulations

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Consumer Buying Behaviour (CBB)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU/Kotler Framework):** *Consumer Buying Behaviour* is the study of how individuals, groups, or organizations select, buy, use, and dispose of goods, services, ideas, or experiences to satisfy needs and wants. In the agro-industrial context, it specifically refers to the decision-making process followed by end-consumers and industrial buyers (e.g., food processors, exporters, retailers) when purchasing agricultural produce, processed foods, or agro-inputs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy (The Farmer's Mango Example):**
> Imagine a farmer in Wayanad harvesting **Alphonso mangoes**. Why do urban customers in Kochi pay ₹400/kg for his organically-certified mangoes when a non-organic one sells for ₹180/kg? The answer lies in CBB. The farmer understands that his target buyer (a health-conscious, upper-middle-class family) is influenced by *culture* (organic is "modern"), *social class* (status symbol of buying organic), *personal factors* (lifestyle), and *psychological triggers* (perceived quality, trust in the "organic" label). CBB is essentially the **science of "Why customers say Yes"**.

### 1.2 Export Trade & Government Regulations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** *Export Trade* refers to the sale of goods/services produced in one country to buyers located in another country, governed by a layered framework of domestic laws, bilateral treaties, and multilateral agreements. *Government Regulations* in this context are the codified rules issued by authorities such as the **Directorate General of Foreign Trade (DGFT)**, **FSSAI**, **APEDA**, **Customs**, and **Ministry of Commerce** that control, monitor, and facilitate agro-exports.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy (The Kerala Spices Exporter's Passport):**
> Think of exporting **Kerala cardamom** to Dubai like an international traveller needing a *passport and visa*. The cardamom consignment cannot simply "board the ship". It needs: (1) an **IEC code** (passport), (2) a **Phytosanitary Certificate** (visa), (3) **FSSAI compliance** (health clearance), (4) **Customs clearance** (immigration stamp), and (5) an **e-Certificate of Origin** (boarding pass). Government regulations are these mandatory checkpoints.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Bodies (Highlight Bold)

- **Market Potential Index (MPI)** – Used to rank export markets for agro-products.
- **FOB (Free on Board) Value** – The standard valuation metric for Indian exports reported to DGFT.
- **HSN (Harmonized System of Nomenclature) Code** – A **6-digit** international standard for classifying traded goods.
- **Major Regulatory Bodies (Agro-Export Context):** **DGFT**, **APEDA**, **FSSAI**, **EIC**, **Plant Quarantine**, **EIC**, **Customs (CBIC)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Pillars of Factors Influencing Consumer Buying Behaviour

The classic **Kotler Framework** groups influencing factors into **four major categories**. Each operates at a different "depth" of the consumer's mind:

#### 2.1.1 Cultural Factors (The Deepest Layer)
- **Culture:** The most fundamental driver. E.g., in Kerala, the cultural preference for *matta rice* over *basmati*.
- **Subculture:** Religion, regional identity (e.g., Onam Sadya traditions drive seasonal demand for specific vegetables).
- **Social Class:** Measured by a combination of occupation, income, education, and wealth. Drives consumption of premium organic produce.

#### 2.1.2 Social Factors (Interpersonal Layer)
- **Reference Groups:** Family, friends, peer farmers (e.g., Farmer Producer Organisations influencing adoption of a new seed variety).
- **Family:** The most influential consumer-buying organization. Family life-cycle stages (Bachelor → Newly Married → Full Nest → Empty Nest) dictate product preferences.
- **Roles and Status:** A person's position in society dictates purchase behaviour. A "Secretary of an FPO" buys different agri-inputs than a casual farmer.

#### 2.1.3 Personal Factors (Individual Layer)
- **Age and Life-Cycle Stage.**
- **Occupation** (e.g., a teacher vs. a daily-wage labourer).
- **Economic Situation:** Disposable income, savings, assets, debt.
- **Lifestyle:** A person's pattern of living expressed in activities, interests, and opinions (AIO).
- **Personality and Self-Concept.**

#### 2.1.4 Psychological Factors (The Core Layer)
- **Motivation (Maslow's Hierarchy applied to agro-products).**
- **Perception:** Selective attention, distortion, retention.
- **Learning:** Experience-based behaviour change.
- **Beliefs and Attitudes.**

### 2.2 The Buyer Decision Process (Five-Stage Model)

$$\text{Need Recognition} \rightarrow \text{Information Search} \rightarrow \text{Evaluation of Alternatives} \rightarrow \text{Purchase Decision} \rightarrow \text{Post-Purchase Behaviour}$$

### 2.3 Export Trade – Government Regulations Architecture

The regulatory architecture is a **three-tier hierarchy**:

1. **Tier 1 – Policy Level:** Foreign Trade Policy (FTP) by **DGFT**, Foreign Exchange Management Act (**FEMA**) by **RBI**.
2. **Tier 2 – Product & Quality Level:** **FSSAI** (Food Safety), **APEDA** (Agricultural & Processed Food Products Export Development Authority), **EIC** (Export Inspection Council), Plant Quarantine (under Ministry of Agriculture).
3. **Tier 3 – Operational Level:** Customs Act by **CBIC**, GST (indirect tax refunds), Logistics and shipping compliance.

### 2.4 KTU Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is a high-yield revision matrix. Memorize this for the 14-mark module questions.

| **Framework / Equation** | **Description / Boundary Conditions** | **Unit / Boundary Value** | **Engineering/Agribusiness Utility** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Consumer Decision Process | 5 stages: Need → Search → Evaluate → Purchase → Post-purchase | Number of stages = **5** | Designing marketing mix for new agro-product launch |
| AIDA Model | Attention → Interest → Desire → Action | Sequence of cognitive states | Designing advertising for value-added agro products |
| Stimulus-Response Model (S-R) | $\text{Buyer} = f(\text{Stimuli}) + \text{Buyer Characteristics}$ | Inputs: Marketing + Environmental | Predicting agri-input sales response |
| Engel-Kollat-Blackwell (EKB) Model | Problem recognition → Search → Alternative evaluation → Choice → Outcome | 5-stage individual decision model | Used in retail buyer behaviour research |
| FOB Value | Price of goods at port of shipment, including all costs up to vessel loading | Currency (₹/USD) | Mandatory for DGFT export reporting |
| CIF Value | Cost + Insurance + Freight to destination port | Currency (₹/USD) | Used in Letter of Credit transactions |
| Export Realisation Ratio (ERR) | $ERR = \frac{\text{Actual Realisation}}{\text{FOB Value}} \times 100$ | Percentage | $\geq 90\%$ is the DGFT benchmark |
| DEPB / RODTEP Benefit | Duty drawback % on FOB value | Percentage | Reduces effective export cost |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** For an agro-entrepreneur planning a **frozen jackfruit export unit** in Thrissur, mastering these factors means understanding: (i) **who buys** (Cultural/Social factors), (ii) **what triggers the purchase** (Psychological factors), and (iii) **what paperwork is needed** (DGFT/APEDA/FSSAI/Customs regulations).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Derivations

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation of the Buyer Behaviour Equation

The Stimulus-Response (S-R) model of consumer behaviour is mathematically expressed as a multi-variable function:

$$\text{Buyer Response} = f(\text{Marketing Stimuli}, \text{Environmental Stimuli}, \text{Buyer Characteristics})$$

**Step-by-Step Expansion:**

The Buyer's "Black Box" is decomposed as follows:

$$\text{Marketing Stimuli} = \{4P\} = \{\text{Product}, \text{Price}, \text{Place}, \text{Promotion}\}$$

$$\text{Environmental Stimuli} = \{C, T, E, P\}$$
where $C = \text{Culture}$, $T = \text{Technology}$, $E = \text{Economy}$, $P = \text{Politics/Law}$

$$\text{Buyer Characteristics} = \{C, S, P, Ps\}$$
where $C = \text{Cultural}$, $S = \text{Social}$, $P = \text{Personal}$, $Ps = \text{Psychological}$

**Combined Functional Form:**

$$\boxed{B = f(\underbrace{4P}_{\text{Marketing}}, \underbrace{C, T, E, P}_{\text{Environment}}, \underbrace{C, S, P, Ps}_{\text{Buyer}})}$$

**Worked Numerical Example (Agro-Export Case Study):**

A Kochi-based spice exporter wants to compute the **Export Realisation Ratio (ERR)** for a consignment of ₹50,00,000 FOB value, where actual realisation after foreign bank charges is ₹46,00,000.

**Step 1:** Identify the variables from the formula sheet.

$$FOB = ₹50,00,000$$
$$\text{Actual Realisation} = ₹46,00,000$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the ERR formula.

$$ERR = \frac{\text{Actual Realisation}}{\text{FOB Value}} \times 100$$

$$ERR = \frac{46,00,000}{50,00,000} \times 100$$

**Step 3:** Perform the division.

$$ERR = 0.92 \times 100$$

**Step 4:** Final evaluation.

$$ERR = 92\%$$

**Interpretation:**

> [!NOTE]
> Since the computed **92% ≥ 90% (DGFT benchmark)**, the consignment qualifies as a satisfactory export realisation and will not attract scrutiny under the **Foreign Exchange Management Act (FEMA)**. If it were below 90%, the exporter would need to provide justification to the **Authorized Dealer (AD) Bank**.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Matching Factors to Agro-Industry Decision Scenarios

| **Factor Type** | **Specific Factor** | **Agro-Industry Scenario (Kerala Context)** | **Managerial Action Required** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cultural | Sub-culture / Religion | Onam season: spike in demand for banana leaves, jaggery, rice | Pre-season capacity planning by FPO |
| Social | Reference Group | Farmers in Kuttanad adopt SRI method after seeing peers succeed | Demonstration plots by Krishi Vigyan Kendra |
| Personal | Economic Situation | Drop in pepper prices reduces middle-class consumption of branded spice mixes | Reposition product as "value pack" |
| Psychological | Belief | "Organic = Healthy" belief boosts organic turmeric sales | Invest in FSSAI organic certification |
| Govt. Regulation | APEDA Registration | Mandatory for export of scheduled products (fruits, vegetables, processed foods) | Apply via APEDA online portal |
| Govt. Regulation | FSSAI License | Mandatory for any food business operator | BIS/FSSAI registration before commercial sale |
| Govt. Regulation | Plant Quarantine | Mandatory phytosanitary certificate for fresh produce export | Coordinate with PQ Station, Kochi |

### 3.3 Conceptual Derivation: Why Government Regulates Export Trade

The logic for regulation can be derived from four national objectives:

1. **Export Quality Assurance:** Protect India's brand value (e.g., preventing rejection of Indian spices in EU markets).
2. **Foreign Exchange Earnings:** Ensure timely repatriation of export proceeds.
3. **Food Security & Strategic Reserves:** Restrict export of essential commodities (e.g., onion, rice during scarcity).
4. **Compliance with WTO / SPS / TBT Agreements:** Meet international standards to retain market access.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
