<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define a Project Report. List any four essential components of a project report for an agro-industrial unit.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Project Report** is a written document that presents a detailed plan for setting up an agro-industrial enterprise, covering technical, financial, market, and legal aspects. It acts as a bridge between the business idea and actual implementation.

**Four Essential Components:**

1. **Introduction & Objectives** – Defines the purpose and scope of the project.
2. **Technical Plan** – Describes the production process, machinery, and plant layout.
3. **Financial Analysis** – Includes cost of production, BEP, PBP, NPV, IRR.
4. **Market Analysis** – Covers demand, supply, competitors, and marketing strategy.

*(1 mark for definition + 0.5 mark × 4 = 2 marks for components = 3 marks total)*

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is Break-Even Point (BEP)? Why is it considered a vital parameter in a project report?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Break-Even Point (BEP)** is the level of production or sales at which **total revenue equals total cost**, i.e., the project makes **neither profit nor loss**.

$$
\text{BEP} \, (\%) = \dfrac{\text{Fixed Cost}}{\text{Fixed Cost} + \text{Contribution}} \times 100
$$

**Importance in a Project Report:**

- Indicates the **minimum capacity utilization** required to avoid losses.
- A **lower BEP** (< 60%) signifies a **safer investment** with built-in cushion against market downturns.
- Helps **bankers and investors** assess **risk exposure** of the proposed agro-industrial venture.
- Guides decisions on **pricing, cost control, and capacity planning**.

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for formula + 1 mark for importance = 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Internal Choice

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, Part B carries **14 marks** with **internal choice**. Students must answer **either Option A or Option B in full**. Each option has two sub-parts (a) and (b), carrying **7 marks each**.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **step-by-step procedure for the preparation of a project report** for setting up a small-scale agro-processing unit (e.g., a fruit processing or rice mill unit). **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

The preparation of a project report involves the following sequential steps:

**Step 1 – Identification of the Business Opportunity**
The entrepreneur identifies a gap in the agro-value chain (e.g., unprocessed mangoes, low rice milling capacity in the region). *(0.5 mark)*

**Step 2 – Conducting Feasibility Study**
Three feasibility tests are performed: **Market Feasibility** (demand-supply gap), **Technical Feasibility** (machinery availability), and **Financial Feasibility** (rough cost-benefit). *(1 mark)*

**Step 3 – Data Collection**
Primary data is collected via field visits and personal interviews; secondary data via government publications, NABARD reports, and market surveys. *(0.5 mark)*

**Step 4 – Drafting the Project Profile**
A one-page summary card is prepared with the **name of the unit, promoters, location, product, installed capacity, and total project cost**. *(0.5 mark)*

**Step 5 – Preparing the Technical Plan**
This includes a **process flow diagram** (Raw Material → Cleaning → Processing → Packaging), **plant layout**, and a **machinery list with costs**. *(1 mark)*

**Step 6 – Financial Projections**
Key financial metrics are calculated: **Total Project Cost (TPC), Cost of Production (COP), Break-Even Point (BEP), Pay-Back Period (PBP), Net Present Value (NPV), and Internal Rate of Return (IRR)**. *(1.5 marks)*

**Step 7 – Manpower and Organizational Plan**
Define the workforce — skilled, semi-skilled, and unskilled — along with salary structure and organizational hierarchy. *(0.5 mark)*

**Step 8 – Statutory and Legal Requirements**
List all required **licenses and clearances**: FSSAI, GST, Factory License, Pollution NOC, Trade License, Electricity connection. *(0.5 mark)*

**Step 9 – Risk and SWOT Analysis**
Identify Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, Threats. *(0.5 mark)*

**Step 10 – Compilation and Submission**
The report is finalized with annexures, references, and submitted to the **bank, NABARD, or government agency** for financial assistance. *(0.5 mark)*

---

**(b)** A proposed agro-industrial unit requires a **Fixed Capital of ₹ 200 Lakhs** and a **Working Capital of ₹ 60 Lakhs**. The **annual sales revenue is ₹ 480 Lakhs** and the **annual cost of production is ₹ 410 Lakhs**. The **fixed cost component is ₹ 120 Lakhs** and the **variable cost component is ₹ 290 Lakhs**. Calculate: **(i) Total Project Cost, (ii) Break-Even Point (%), (iii) Pay-Back Period** assuming the **annual cash inflow after tax is ₹ 55 Lakhs**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer with Step-by-Step Calculation:**

**Given Data:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FC} &= ₹ \, 200 \, \text{Lakhs} \\
\text{WC} &= ₹ \, 60 \, \text{Lakhs} \\
\text{SR} &= ₹ \, 480 \, \text{Lakhs} \\
\text{COP} &= ₹ \, 410 \, \text{Lakhs} \\
\text{Fixed Cost} &= ₹ \, 120 \, \text{Lakhs} \\
\text{Variable Cost} &= ₹ \, 290 \, \text{Lakhs} \\
\text{Cash Inflow after Tax} &= ₹ \, 55 \, \text{Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Total Project Cost (TPC):** *(2 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TPC} &= \text{FC} + \text{WC} \\
\text{TPC} &= 200 + 60 \\
\text{TPC} &= 260 \, \text{Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Contribution (C):** *(1 mark)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C} &= \text{SR} - \text{Variable Cost} \\
\text{C} &= 480 - 290 = 190 \, \text{Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Break-Even Point (%):** *(2 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BEP} \, (\%) &= \dfrac{\text{Fixed Cost}}{\text{Fixed Cost} + \text{Contribution}} \times 100 \\
\text{BEP} \, (\%) &= \dfrac{120}{120 + 190} \times 100 \\
\text{BEP} \, (\%) &= \dfrac{120}{310} \times 100 \\
\text{BEP} \, (\%) &= 38.71\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The unit needs to operate at only **38.71% of installed capacity** to break even — indicating **low risk**. *(0.5 mark)*

**Step 4 — Pay-Back Period (PBP):** *(1.5 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PBP} &= \dfrac{\text{Total Investment}}{\text{Annual Cash Inflow after Tax}} \\
\text{PBP} &= \dfrac{260}{55} \\
\text{PBP} &= 4.727 \, \text{years} \approx 4 \, \text{years and 9 months}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The investment is recovered in **~4 years 9 months**, which is **within the acceptable 5-year benchmark** for agro-industries. *(0.5 mark)*

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023 – Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** Discuss the **structure of a project report** for an agro-industrial venture. Enumerate the **contents of the financial analysis section** with relevant formulas. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Structure of a Project Report:**

1. **Cover Page** – Title, name of promoter, location, date. *(0.5 mark)*
2. **Executive Summary** – 1–2 page snapshot. *(0.5 mark)*
3. **Introduction & Project Background** – Origin, promoter profile. *(0.5 mark)*
4. **Objectives** – Primary and secondary goals. *(0.5 mark)*
5. **Justification** – Why the project is needed. *(0.5 mark)*
6. **Product Profile & Technical Plan** – Process flow, plant layout, machinery. *(0.5 mark)*
7. **Market Analysis** – Demand-supply, competitors, marketing mix. *(0.5 mark)*
8. **Location & Site Analysis** – Why the site was selected. *(0.5 mark)*
9. **Manpower Plan** – Organizational hierarchy, salary structure. *(0.5 mark)*
10. **Financial Analysis** – Detailed below. *(0.5 mark)*
11. **Statutory & Environmental Compliance** – Licenses, NOCs. *(0.5 mark)*
12. **SWOT / Risk Analysis & Annexures.** *(0.5 mark)*

**Contents of the Financial Analysis Section (with formulas):** *(1 mark for listing + 1.5 marks for formulas)*

- **Total Project Cost (TPC)** = Fixed Capital + Working Capital
- **Cost of Production (COP)** = Raw Material + Labour + Utilities + Depreciation + Overheads
- **Break-Even Point (BEP %)** = $\dfrac{\text{Fixed Cost}}{\text{Fixed Cost} + \text{Contribution}} \times 100$
- **Pay-Back Period (PBP)** = $\dfrac{\text{Total Investment}}{\text{Annual Cash Inflow after Tax}}$
- **Net Present Value (NPV)** = $\sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{\text{CF}_{t}}{(1+r)^{t}}$
- **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** solved when NPV = 0
- **Debt-Equity Ratio** = $\dfrac{\text{Total Debt}}{\text{Total Equity}}$
- **Return on Investment (ROI)** = $\dfrac{\text{Net Profit}}{\text{Total Investment}} \times 100$

---

**(b)** A **rice mill project** has the following financial details. Compute the **Net Present Value (NPV)** at a **discount rate of 10%**, given **equal annual cash inflows of ₹ 40 Lakhs for 8 years** and an **initial investment of ₹ 220 Lakhs**. Comment on the viability. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Initial Investment (CF}_0\text{)} &= ₹ \, 220 \, \text{Lakhs} \\
\text{Annual Cash Inflow (CF}_t\text{)} &= ₹ \, 40 \, \text{Lakhs (constant, for } t = 1 \text{ to } 8) \\
\text{Discount Rate (r)} &= 10\% = 0.10 \\
\text{Project Life (n)} &= 8 \, \text{years}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Discount Factor (Present Value Annuity Factor, PVAF):** *(2 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PVAF} &= \dfrac{1 - (1 + r)^{-n}}{r} \\
\text{PVAF} &= \dfrac{1 - (1.10)^{-8}}{0.10} \\
\text{PVAF} &= \dfrac{1 - 0.4665}{0.10} \\
\text{PVAF} &= \dfrac{0.5335}{0.10} \\
\text{PVAF} &= 5.3349
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Present Value of Cash Inflows:** *(1.5 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PV of Inflows} &= \text{Annual Cash Inflow} \times \text{PVAF} \\
\text{PV of Inflows} &= 40 \times 5.3349 \\
\text{PV of Inflows} &= 213.40 \, \text{Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Net Present Value (NPV):** *(1.5 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NPV} &= \text{PV of Inflows} - \text{Initial Investment} \\
\text{NPV} &= 213.40 - 220 \\
\text{NPV} &= -6.60 \, \text{Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Comment on Viability:** *(2 marks)*

Since **NPV is negative (-₹ 6.60 Lakhs)** at a **10% discount rate**, the project is **NOT financially viable at the assumed cash inflow level**. The project would be acceptable if:

- The annual cash inflow increases to **at least ₹ 41.24 Lakhs** (220 ÷ 5.3349 ≈ 41.24), OR
- The discount rate falls to approximately **8.5%** (where NPV = 0), OR
- The initial investment is reduced below **₹ 213.40 Lakhs**.

**Recommendation:** The entrepreneur should either **renegotiate the loan** for a lower interest rate, **seek subsidy under PMKSY/MIDH**, or **improve operational efficiency** to enhance cash inflows.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Where Students Lose Marks):**
> 1. **Confusing BEP units** — BEP must be expressed in **% of installed capacity**, not in absolute units (₹ Lakhs). Marks deducted for wrong unit.
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract Initial Investment in NPV** — Many students only calculate PV of inflows and forget the `-CF₀` term. This costs **1.5 to 2 marks** instantly.
> 3. **Ignoring comment/interpretation** — Numerical answers without a concluding remark (e.g., "project is viable / not viable") fetch **partial marks only**.
> 4. **Incomplete structure listing** — A project report has **12 sections**; listing only 4-5 invites a **3-mark penalty**.
> 5. **Wrong formula for Pay-Back Period** — Using Net Profit instead of **Cash Inflow after Tax** is a common error.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- A **Project Report** is a **comprehensive written blueprint** covering technical, financial, market, and legal aspects of an agro-industrial venture.
- It contains **12 standard sections** from Cover Page to Annexures, following a **funnel approach** (broad → specific).
- **Total Project Cost (TPC)** = Fixed Capital + Working Capital — the **first formula** to compute in any report.
- **Break-Even Point (BEP)** must be **< 60%** of installed capacity for an agro-project to be considered **low-risk**.
- **Pay-Back Period (PBP)** should ideally be **< 5 years** for agro-industrial units.
- **NPV > 0** → Project is **acceptable**; **NPV < 0** → Project should be **rejected** at the given discount rate.
- **IRR > Cost of Capital** → Project is **viable**; this is the **gold standard** test of profitability.
- A project report is **mandatory** for **bank loan sanction, NABARD subsidy, and government approvals** in India.
- The **10-step preparation process** flows from Opportunity Identification → Feasibility Study → Data Collection → Profile → Technical Plan → Financial Projections → Manpower → Statutory → SWOT → Compilation.
- **SWOT Analysis** (Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, Threats) is the **final qualitative check** before submission.
- For KTU exams, **always present numerical solutions in a structured, step-by-step format** with **formula → substitution → result → interpretation**.
- **FSSAI, GST, Factory License, and Pollution NOC** are the **four most common statutory clearances** for agro-industrial units in India.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# MODULE 4: MARKET FEASIBILITY REPORTS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)**
> A **Market Feasibility Report (MFR)** is a structured analytical document that systematically evaluates the commercial viability of a proposed agro-industrial project by assessing the prevailing market conditions, demand–supply dynamics, competitive landscape, target customer segments, pricing strategies, distribution channels, and projected sales volumes over the operational life of the project. It forms the **commercial pillar** of the broader Feasibility Study Report (FSR) and precedes the technical, financial, and managerial feasibility analyses in the project appraisal sequence.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Farmer's Market Compass"**
> Imagine a farmer in Palakkad who wants to start a small-scale tapioca processing unit. Before investing ₹50 lakhs, he would naturally ask:
> - *Is there enough demand for tapioca flour in Kerala?* (Market Size)
> - *Who are my competitors selling similar products?* (Competition Analysis)
> - *At what price can I sell my product and still make a profit?* (Pricing & Revenue Forecasting)
> - *Which supermarkets or wholesale dealers will buy from me?* (Distribution Channels)
>
> A **Market Feasibility Report** is exactly this — but written systematically, supported with numbers, charts, and validated through primary (field surveys) and secondary (government databases) research. It is the **compass** that tells the agro-entrepreneur whether the proposed business will sail profitably or sink before launch.

### 1.2 Strategic Position in the Feasibility Hierarchy

| Stage | Feasibility Type | Core Question Answered | Typical Section in Report |
|-------|------------------|------------------------|---------------------------|
| 1 | **Market Feasibility** | *Will the product sell?* | Module 4 Focus |
| 2 | Technical Feasibility | *Can we produce it?* | Module 5 |
| 3 | Financial Feasibility | *Will it be profitable?* | Module 6 |
| 4 | Managerial Feasibility | *Can we manage it?* | Module 7 |

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics Used in MFR

- **TAM (Total Addressable Market)** — *the entire revenue opportunity available*
- **SAM (Serviceable Available Market)** — *the segment you can realistically serve*
- **SOM (Serviceable Obtainable Market)** — *the share you can realistically capture*
- **CAGR (Compound Annual Growth Rate)** — *standard 5-year growth metric*
- **Market Penetration Ratio** — *actual sales ÷ potential sales*

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The MFR is evaluated for **15 marks** in the End Semester Examination under Part B (14-mark questions often originate from this module), and is also tested through 3-mark Part A short questions on definitions, market size calculation methods, and the structure of a feasibility report.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Objectives of a Market Feasibility Report

An MFR is prepared to accomplish the following six (6) core objectives, each of which directly maps to a KTU Course Outcome (CO):

1. **Demand Assessment** — Quantify current and future demand for the proposed agro-product.
2. **Supply Mapping** — Identify existing producers, their capacities, and market shares.
3. **Competitive Intelligence** — Analyse strengths and weaknesses of rival firms.
4. **Customer Profiling** — Segment buyers by demographics, geography, and behaviour.
5. **Pricing Strategy Formulation** — Determine price points that balance affordability and profitability.
6. **Risk Identification** — Highlight market-side risks (price volatility, demand collapse, substitute products).

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Structural Logic of MFR Preparation

The preparation of an MFR follows a **five-stage sequential logic**:

> **Step 1: Define the Product & Geographic Boundary**
> Clearly articulate what the agro-product is (e.g., cold-pressed coconut oil), the geographic scope (Kerala, South India, Export), and the target customer (B2C retail vs. B2B food processors).

> **Step 2: Conduct Secondary Research**
> Gather data from **APEDA, NABARD, Ministry of Agriculture, state horticulture boards, FAO, and industry associations** to establish baseline market size, historical growth rates, and trade statistics.

> **Step 3: Conduct Primary Research**
> Use **structured questionnaires, focus group discussions (FGDs), and key-informant interviews** with farmers, wholesalers, retailers, and consumers. Sample size typically follows the formula:
>
> $$n = \frac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{e^2}$$
>
> where **Z = 1.96** (95% confidence), **p = 0.5** (assumed proportion), **e = margin of error**.

> **Step 4: Analyse Demand–Supply Gap**
> Compare projected demand against existing supply to identify the *market gap* the proposed unit will fill.

> **Step 5: Forecast Sales & Recommend Marketing Mix**
> Build 5-year sales projections using **trend extrapolation, regression analysis, or bottom-up market share models**, and recommend the **4 Ps** of marketing.

## 2.3 The Classic 4 Ps Marketing Mix for Agro-Products

| Element | Strategic Question | Agro-Industry Example |
|---------|-------------------|------------------------|
| **Product** | What variety, grade, and packaging? | Organic turmeric powder, 100g pouches |
| **Price** | Penetration, skimming, or parity pricing? | ₹180/kg (10% below market leader) |
| **Place** | Distribution channel — direct, retail, export? | Super markets + online (Amazon, Flipkart) |
| **Promotion** | Advertising, branding, trade fairs? | Krishi Melas, social media ads |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Formulas for Numerical Problems in MFR (KTU Exam-Ready)**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Utility |
|---|---------|----------------|---------------------|
| 1 | $\text{CAGR} = \left(\dfrac{V_f}{V_i}\right)^{\frac{1}{n}} - 1$ | $V_f$ = final value, $V_i$ = initial value, $n$ = years | Forecasts 5-year market growth |
| 2 | $\text{TAM} = N \times A \times U \times P$ | $N$ = no. of buyers, $A$ = adoption %, $U$ = units/year, $P$ = price | Top-down market sizing |
| 3 | $n = \dfrac{Z^2 \cdot p(1-p)}{e^2}$ | $Z$ = Z-score, $p$ = proportion, $e$ = error margin | Sample size for surveys |
| 4 | $\text{Market Share \%} = \dfrac{\text{Company Sales}}{\text{Total Industry Sales}} \times 100$ | Percentage capture in a defined market | Competitive positioning |
| 5 | $D_e = \dfrac{\%\Delta Q_d}{\%\Delta P}$ | Price elasticity of demand | Pricing sensitivity testing |
| 6 | $\text{MA}(k) = \dfrac{X_{t} + X_{t-1} + \cdots + X_{t-k+1}}{k}$ | $k$-period Moving Average | Demand smoothing/forecasting |
| 7 | $F_{t+1} = \alpha X_t + (1-\alpha)F_t$ | $\alpha$ = smoothing constant, $0 < \alpha < 1$ | Exponential smoothing forecast |
| 8 | $\text{Sales Forecast} = \text{Market Size} \times \text{Penetration \%} \times \text{Realisation \%}$ | Composite top-down forecast | Revenue projection in MFR |

> [!NOTE]
> **LaTeX Note for KTU Students:** In exam answer sheets, write these formulas using standard mathematical notation. Avoid the vertical pipe symbol `|` for absolute value in tables (use $\vert$ or $\mid$ instead, as per the KTU evaluation rubric for clarity).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering & Management Utility

The MFR is the *single most critical document* presented to:

- **Banks & Financial Institutions** (for loan appraisal)
- **NABARD / SFAC / MoFPI** (for subsidy schemes like PMKSY, PMFME)
- **Venture Capitalists & Angel Investors**
- **State Industrial Development Corporations** (KSIDC, KSIDC Kerala)
- **Internal Board of Directors** of agro-firms

Without a credible MFR, no financial sanction is granted — making this module a **high-weightage area** in both academics and practice.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Market Size (TAM) Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-Style 14-Mark Question)**
> An entrepreneur plans to launch a packaged **cold-pressed groundnut oil** brand in Kerala. The target population of Kerala is **3.5 crore**. Market research indicates:
> - **35%** of households consume branded edible oils.
> - Average annual consumption per adopting household = **15 kg**.
> - Realisable price (after discounts) = **₹280 per kg**.
> - Expected market share after 3 years = **4%**.
>
> Calculate: **(a)** TAM, **(b)** SAM (assume Kerala only), **(c)** SOM (target capture), and **(d)** Expected annual revenue.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given Data:**
- Total population (N) = 3.5 crore = $3.5 \times 10^7$
- Adoption rate (A) = 35% = 0.35
- Units per year (U) = 15 kg
- Price per unit (P) = ₹280
- Target market share = 4% = 0.04

**Part (a) — Total Addressable Market (TAM):**

Using the formula:

$$\text{TAM} = N \times A \times U \times P$$

Substituting the values:

$$\text{TAM} = (3.5 \times 10^7) \times 0.35 \times 15 \times 280$$

Let us compute step by step:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & N \times A = (3.5 \times 10^7) \times 0.35 \\
& = 1.225 \times 10^7 \text{ adopting households (approx.)} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & (1.225 \times 10^7) \times 15 \\
& = 1.8375 \times 10^8 \text{ kg/year total consumption} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & (1.8375 \times 10^8) \times 280 \\
& = 5.145 \times 10^{10} \\
\text{TAM} \quad & = ₹ 5,145 \text{ crore (approx.)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (b) — Serviceable Available Market (SAM):**
Assume the brand is initially launched only in **Kerala**, so SAM = TAM in this case:

$$\text{SAM} = ₹ 5{,}145 \text{ crore}$$

**Part (c) — Serviceable Obtainable Market (SOM):**
Apply the 4% market share assumption:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{SOM} & = \text{SAM} \times \text{Market Share \%} \\
& = 5{,}145 \times 0.04 \\
& = ₹ 205.8 \text{ crore}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (d) — Expected Annual Revenue:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Revenue} & = \text{SOM in units} \times \text{Price} \\
\text{SOM (units)} & = (1.8375 \times 10^8) \times 0.04 = 7.35 \times 10^6 \text{ kg} \\
\text{Revenue} & = (7.35 \times 10^6) \times 280 \\
& = ₹ 205.8 \text{ crore (matches SOM value)}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Notice the **units discipline** — TAM is expressed in **rupees**, but the underlying logic works in *kilograms consumed*. Always show the intermediate step (volume in kg) before multiplying by price. Many students lose 2 marks by directly multiplying percentages without converting units.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: CAGR Calculation for 5-Year Demand Forecast

> **Problem Statement**
> The market for organic spices in India was **₹2,400 crore** in 2020 and is expected to reach **₹4,200 crore** by 2025. Calculate the **Compound Annual Growth Rate (CAGR)**.

### Solution

**Given:**
- Initial value $(V_i)$ = ₹2,400 crore
- Final value $(V_f)$ = ₹4,200 crore
- Number of years $(n)$ = 5

Applying the CAGR formula:

$$\text{CAGR} = \left(\frac{V_f}{V_i}\right)^{\frac{1}{n}} - 1$$

Substituting values:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{CAGR} & = \left(\frac{4{,}200}{2{,}400}\right)^{\frac{1}{5}} - 1 \\
& = (1.75)^{0.2} - 1
\end{aligned}$$

To compute $(1.75)^{0.2}$, take the natural logarithm:

$$\begin{aligned}
\ln(1.75) & = 0.5596 \\
0.2 \times 0.5596 & = 0.1119 \\
e^{0.1119} & \approx 1.1184 \\
\text{CAGR} & \approx 1.1184 - 1 = 0.1184 \\
\text{CAGR} & \approx 11.84\% \text{ per annum}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Interpretation:** The organic spices market is growing at nearly **12% annually**, indicating a robust opportunity for a new entrant.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Sample Size Determination for Market Survey

> **Problem Statement**
> A feasibility study for a dairy plant in Thrissur requires a consumer survey. Using a 95% confidence level and 5% margin of error, calculate the minimum sample size.

### Solution

**Given:**
- Confidence level = 95% → **Z = 1.96**
- Assumed proportion $p$ = 0.5 (maximum variance)
- Margin of error $e$ = 0.05

Applying the formula:

$$n = \frac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{e^2}$$

Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
n & = \frac{(1.96)^2 \times 0.5 \times (1-0.5)}{(0.05)^2} \\
& = \frac{3.8416 \times 0.25}{0.0025} \\
& = \frac{0.9604}{0.0025} \\
& = 384.16
\end{aligned}$$

Rounding up (sample size must be a whole number):

$$n = 385 \text{ respondents (minimum)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Note:** Always round **up** the sample size, never round down. Also mention the assumption $p = 0.5$ in your answer — many students forget this and lose a mark.

## 3.4 Worked Example 4: Exponential Smoothing Demand Forecast

> **Problem Statement**
> Monthly sales (in tonnes) of a rice brand for the last 5 months are: **40, 45, 38, 50, 48**. Using exponential smoothing with $\alpha = 0.3$ and $F_1 = 40$, forecast the sales for month 6.

### Solution

The exponential smoothing formula:

$$F_{t+1} = \alpha X_t + (1-\alpha) F_t$$

Computing sequentially:

$$\begin{aligned}
F_2 & = 0.3 \times 40 + 0.7 \times 40 = 40.00 \\
F_3 & = 0.3 \times 45 + 0.7 \times 40 = 13.5 + 28.0 = 41.50 \\
F_4 & = 0.3 \times 38 + 0.7 \times 41.50 = 11.4 + 29.05 = 40.45 \\
F_5 & = 0.3 \times 50 + 0.7 \times 40.45 = 15.0 + 28.315 = 43.315 \\
F_6 & = 0.3 \times 48 + 0.7 \times 43.315 = 14.4 + 30.3205 = 44.7205 \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Forecast for Month 6** $\approx$ **44.72 tonnes**

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** When $\alpha$ is small (0.3), the forecast is *smoothed heavily* and is less reactive to recent shocks. Agro-products with seasonal volatility often warrant smaller $\alpha$ values (0.1–0.3).
